EDCS-22800162.translation: The state of Saeponensium gave a triumphal statue by decree of the decurions to the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Optimus Augustus, Germanicus, Dacian, Parthian, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 18th time, acclaimed imperator for the 10th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the country.
EDCS-22800162.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a triumphal statue by the community of Saeponensium to Emperor Trajan, likely around 114 CE, highlighting his numerous military victories and consulships.

EDCS-22800333.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription fragment that mentions the title 'Augustus', likely referring to an emperor, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900239.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae Valeria Qu Valeri Valentis filia ad cultum operis a patre optumo sic exornati in honorem corporis oleariorum dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-23900239.translation: To Minerva Augusta, Valeria Qu, daughter of Valerius Valentis, dedicated and consecrated [this] for the worship of the work, so excellently adorned by her excellent father, in honor of the association of oil merchants.
EDCS-23900239.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Minerva Augusta by Valeria Qu, daughter of Valerius Valentis, in honor of the oil merchants' guild, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900240.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Clodius Marion nnorum LXXXX hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-23900240.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Clodius lived 90 years. He is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-23900240.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording the name, age, and a common funerary wish for the deceased Marcus Clodius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900243.text_edition: \n Aeliae Luci filiae Postumi Labeonis amici\n
EDCS-23900243.translation: To Aelia, daughter of Lucius, a friend of Postumus Labeo.
EDCS-23900243.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aelia, daughter of Lucius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Mercurialis vixit nnos LXX hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-23900244.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Mercurialis lived 70 years, he is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-23900244.summary: This is a tombstone for Mercurialis, who lived 70 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900245.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Primigenius servus nnorum XXV pius in suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-23900245.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Primigenius, a dutiful slave, lived 25 years, he is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-23900245.summary: This is a tombstone for Primigenius, a slave who lived 25 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900246.text_edition: \n Iole Grusiae Secundae annorum XXXXII hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-23900246.translation: Iole, daughter of Grusia Secunda, 42 years old, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-23900246.summary: This is a tombstone for Iole, who lived 42 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24601060.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Cornelius Nicon, 52 years old, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you. Cornelius Optatus made (this) for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-24601060.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Cornelius Optatus for his father, Lucius Cornelius Nicon, who was 52 years old, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300369.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Sempronius Ma nnum XX pius in suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-28300369.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sempronius Ma[---], 20 years old, a dutiful person in his household, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-28300369.summary: This is a tombstone for Sempronius Ma[---], who was 20 years old, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28300374.text_edition: \n Eponae Lucius Satrius Probus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28300374.translation: Lucius Satrius Probus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Epona.
EDCS-28300374.summary: This is a votive offering to the goddess Epona, dedicated by Lucius Satrius Probus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400023.translation: Saturnina, daughter of Asprenas, 4 years old, is buried here. Caenea, daughter of Cam[alus?], 20 years old.
EDCS-28400023.summary: This is a tombstone recording the burials of Saturnina, daughter of Asprenas (4 years old), and Caenea, daughter of Cam[alus?] (20 years old), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400025.text_edition: \n Caius Norbanus Cai filius Rusticus annorum LXX hic situs est sit tibi terra levis Caius Domitius Sturninus de suo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28400025.translation: Caius Norbanus Rusticus, son of Caius, 70 years old, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you. Caius Domitius Sturninus took care to have this made at his own expense.
EDCS-28400025.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Norbanus Rusticus, who lived 70 years, erected at the expense of Caius Domitius Sturninus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400402.text_edition: \n Lucius Fabius Fabullus hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-28400402.translation: Lucius Fabius Fabullus is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you.
EDCS-28400402.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Fabius Fabullus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400406.text_edition: \n Cornelia Maurilla annorum XXXVII cara suis hic sita est\n
EDCS-28400406.translation: Cornelia Maurilla, aged 37, dear to her family, is buried here.
EDCS-28400406.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Cornelia Maurilla.

EDCS-28400407.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cornelius Chryseros nnorum XXVIIII karus <i=L>(n) suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-28400407.translation: Dedicated to the Divine Spirits. Cornelius Chryseros, aged 29, dear to his family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-28400407.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Cornelius Chryseros, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400408.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Ampliatus annorum LX <c=G>(arus) suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-28400408.translation: Lucius Valerius Ampliatus, aged 60, dear to his family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-28400408.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Ampliatus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400424.text_edition: \n Quintus Valerius Anchialus situs est nnum LX\n
EDCS-28400424.translation: Quintus Valerius Anchialus is buried here, aged 60.
EDCS-28400424.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Valerius Anchialus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-30000128.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Kapitolino Conservatori generis humani Sempronia Flaviana Luci liberta votum solvit\n
EDCS-30000128.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus, preserver of the human race. Sempronia Flaviana, freedwoman of Lucius, fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-30000128.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Sempronia Flaviana, a freedwoman.

EDCS-30000129.text_edition: \n Quinto Antonio Granio Erasino viro egregio |(centurioni) frumentario canaliculario procuratori ad familiam gladiatoriam per Italiam Aemiliam Transpadum Liguriam Pannonias Dalmatias procuratori XXmae hereditatium provinciarum duarum Baeticae et Lusitaniae Valerius Auggustorum libertus tabularius magistro innocentissimo ob meritis posuit\n
EDCS-30000129.translation: To Quintus Antonius Granius Erasinus, an excellent man, a grain centurion, canal supervisor, procurator for the gladiatorial families throughout Italy, Aemilia, Transpadana, Liguria, Pannonia, Dalmatia, procurator of the 20th imperial inheritance tax for the two provinces of Baetica and Lusitania, Valerius, freedman of the Augusti, archivist, most innocent master, erected this in honor of his merits.
EDCS-30000129.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Valerius for Quintus Antonius Granius Erasinus, honoring his extensive career in various administrative and military roles.

EDCS-30000131.text_edition: \n Caius Sempronius Niger hic situs est tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-30000131.translation: Caius Sempronius Niger is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-30000131.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of Caius Sempronius Niger.

EDCS-30000296.summary: This inscription likely marks the Via Campana, a road near Rome.

EDCS-30000297.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Valeria Rustica.

EDCS-30000298.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Laeta annorum LV pia in suis hic sita est sit tibi terra levis Quintus Valerius Faustinus annorum XXXX pius in suo[s ------\n
EDCS-30000298.translation: Dedicated to the Divine Spirits. Valeria Laeta, aged 55, dutiful to her family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. Quintus Valerius Faustinus, aged 40, dutiful to his family [is buried here].
EDCS-30000298.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph recording the deaths of Valeria Laeta and Quintus Valerius Faustinus.

EDCS-30000310.translation: His freedman Ursus gives this to our Gaius.
EDCS-30000310.summary: This is a dedication from a freedman named Ursus to his patron Gaius.

EDCS-30000313.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Avillius Eutyches Patriciensis nnum XXX pius in suis hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-30000313.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Avillius Eutyches of Patriciensis, aged 30, pious among his own people, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-30000313.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Avillius Eutyches, who died at age 30.

EDCS-31100020.summary: This is the name 'Valerius Larensis' inscribed, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-31100021.summary: This is the name 'Valerius Larensis' inscribed, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-31100022.summary: This is the name 'Valerius Larensis' inscribed, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-31100024.summary: This is the name 'Valerius Larensis' inscribed, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-31100025.summary: This is the name 'Valerius Larensis' inscribed, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-33200034.translation: Gaius Julius Apollonius, son of Tursi, is buried here.
EDCS-33200034.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Apollonius, son of Tursi.

EDCS-33700056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Fabia Rusticula annorum XXXI hic sita est sit tibi terra levis Callistus posuit\n
EDCS-33700056.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Fabia Rusticula, aged 31, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. Callistus set this up.
EDCS-33700056.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fabia Rusticula, aged 31, erected by Callistus.

EDCS-34300602.text_edition: \n Flavia Epis nnorum XXX hic sita est sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-34300602.translation: Flavia Epis, aged 30, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-34300602.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Epis, who died at age 30.

EDCS-34300603.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Decimus Iunius Rusticus.
EDCS-34300603.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Decimus Iunius Rusticus, likely inscribed on his tombstone.

EDCS-34300879.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Aemilia Gymnas, aged seventeen, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34300879.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilia Gymnas, who died at age seventeen, and includes a common Latin wish for the deceased.

EDCS-34300897.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Corneliae Kalles uxsoris sic piisimae\n
EDCS-34300897.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. For Cornelia Kalles, his most pious wife.
EDCS-34300897.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Cornelia Kalles, described as a very pious wife.

EDCS-34300904.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Annula annorum XXIII pia in suis hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-34300904.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Annula, aged twenty-three, pious among her family, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34300904.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Annula, who died at age twenty-three, and includes a common Latin wish for the deceased.

EDCS-36300041.translation: At Betun, Aelia Belesi, by lot and by order, willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-36300041.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aelia Belesi at Betun, possibly to a local deity.

EDCS-36400305.text_edition: \n Valeriae Titi filiae Lucani decreto decurionum Aciliae Luci filiae Publi Aemili Silonis decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-36400305.translation: To Valeria, daughter of Titus, from Lucania, by decree of the decurions; to Acilia, daughter of Lucius, of Publius Aemilius Sillo, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-36400305.summary: This inscription honors two women, Valeria and Acilia, presumably for public service or recognition, as indicated by the 'decree of the decurions'.

EDCS-37600955.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Herenniu[s ------
EDCS-37600955.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication to Lucius Herennius.

EDCS-37600956.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely part of a funerary monument, with legible names like 'Orina'.

EDCS-38100304.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Ruf[---], aged twenty-three.
EDCS-38100304.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary dedication, likely for a person named Ruf-, who died at age twenty-three.

EDCS-38100307.translation: [---]lius Niceros / [---]lius Clemens / undertook by vow / [---] of the mother.
EDCS-38100307.summary: This inscription appears to record a vow made by individuals, possibly related to a mother, with names like Niceros and Clemens.

EDCS-38100308.translation: Procula, daughter of Titus, aged 15 years. (This tomb occupies a space of) 16 square feet.
EDCS-38100308.summary: This is a tombstone for Procula, who died at age 15, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period, indicating the size of the burial plot.

EDCS-38700180.translation: Here lies Nicolaos. On the fifth day of March, in the fourth indiction.
EDCS-38700180.summary: A burial inscription for Nicolaos, dated to the 4th indiction, likely from the Eastern Roman Empire.

EDCS-38901368.summary: A tombstone with the name Severus repeated three times, possibly to emphasize the name or for a single individual named Severus. The date is uncertain.

EDCS-44800452.translation: To Claudia Pia Fidelis, procurator of the emperor's treasury for the grain supply of Puteoli. Publius Perellius Maximus, decurion of the II Wing of Thracians, (dedicated this) by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-44800452.summary: A dedication to Claudia Pia Fidelis, a procurator in Puteoli, by Publius Perellius Maximus, a military decurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-46400006.text_edition: \n Ante diem IIII Eidus Decembres in Palatio in porticu qua<e> est ad Apollinis scribendo adfuerunt Marcus Valerius Marci filius Lemonia Messal<l>inus Caius Ateius Luci filius Aniensis Capito Sextus Pompeius Sexti filius Arnensis Marcus Pompeius Marci filius Teretina Priscus Caius Arrenus Cai filius Galeria Gallus Lucius Nonius Luci filius Pomptina A<sp>renas quaestor Marcus Vinicius Publi filius Poblilia quaestor quod Tiberius Caesar <divi> Augusti filius Augustus pontifex maxumus sic tribunicia potestate XXII consul III designatus IIII ad senatum rettulit qualis causa Cnaei Pisonis <patris> visa esset et an merito sibi mortem conscisse videretur et qualis causa Marci Pisonis visa esset cui relationi adiecisset uti precum suarum pro adulescente memor is ordo esset <et> qualis causa Plancinae visa e<s>set pro qua persona quid petisset et quas propter causas exposuisset antea et quid de Visellio Karo et de Sempronio Basso comitibus Cnaei Pisonis patris iudicaret senatus de iis rebus ita censuerunt senatu <m=S> populu<m=S>que Romanum ante omnia dis immortalibus gratias agere quod nefaris consilis Cnaei Pisonis patris tranquillitatem praesentis status rei publicae quo melior <optari> non pote sic <e=V>t quod beneficio principis nostri frui contigit turbar<i=E> passi non sunt deinde Tiberio Caesari Augusto principi nostro quod earum rerum omnium quae ad explorandam veritatem necessariae fuerunt copiam senatu<i=S> fecerit cuius aequitatem et patientiam hoc quoque nomine admirari se senatum quod <cum> manufestissim<a=VM> sint Cnaei Pisonis patris scelera et ipse de se supplicium sumpsisset nihilominus causa<m> eius cognosci voluerit filiosque eius arcessitos <h>ortatus sit ut patris sui causam defendere<n>t ita ut eum quoque qui ordinis senatori nondum esset ob eam rem introduci in senatum vellet et copiam utr<i>que dicendi pro patre et pro matre ipsorum et pro Marco Pison<e=IM> faceret itaque cum per aliquo<t=D> dies acta causa sit ab accusatoribus Cnaei Pisonis patris et ab ipso Cnaeo Pisone <patre> recitatae epistulae recitata exempularia codicillorum quos Germanicus Caesar Cnaeo Pisoni patri scripsisset producti testes quoiusque sic ii ordinis sint <senatum> a<rbit=DMI>rari singularem moderationem patientiamque Germanici Caesaris evicta<m> esse feritate morum Cnaei Pisonis patris atque ob id morientem Germanicum Caesarem quoius sic mortis fuisse caussam sic Cnaeum Pisonem patrem ipse testatus sit non inmerito amicitiam ei renuntiasse qui cum deberet meminisse adiutorem se datum esse Germanico Caesari qui a principe nostro ex auctoritate huius ordinis ad rerum transmarinarum statum componendum missus esset desiderantium praesentiam aut ipsius Tiberi Caesaris Augusti aut filiorum alterius utrius neclecta sic maiestatem domus Augustae neclecto sic etiam iure publico quod adle<c=G>t<us> pro consule et ei pro consule de quo lex ad populum lata esset ut in qua<mcum>que provinciam venisset m<ai=IN>us ei imperium quam <ei=SIBI> qui eam provinciam proconsule optineret esset dum in omni re m<ai=IN>us imperium Tiberio Cae<s>ar<i=E> Augusto quam Germanico Caesari esset tamquam ipsius arbitri et potestatis <omnia> esse deberent ita se <cum> in provinciam Syriam fuerit gesserit [bellum c]um Armeniacum et Parthicum quantum in ipso fuerit moverit quod neque ex mandatis principis nostri epistulisque frequentibus Germanici Caesaris cum is abesset Vononem qui suspectus regi Parthorum erat longius removeri voluerit ne profugere ex custodia posset id qu<od> fecit et conloqui quosdam ex numero Armeniorum malos et audaces cum Vonone passus sit ut per eosdem tumultus in Armeniam excitaretur ac Vonone<s> vel oc<c>iso vel <ex=IN>pulso rege Armeniae quem Germanicus Caesar ex voluntate patris sui senatus<q>(ue) regem ei genti dedisset <eam> occuparet eaque magnis muneribus Vononis corrupt<u=I>s fecerit bellum etiam civile excitare conatus sit iam pridem numin<e=I> divi Augusti virtutibusque Tiberi Caesaris Augusti omnibus civi<li=BV>s bellis sepultis malis repetendo provinciam <Syriam> post mortem Germanici Caesaris quam vivo eo pessumo et animo et exemplo reliquerat atque ob id milite<s=M> Romani inter se concucurre<re> co<ac=NA>ti s<i=V>nt perspecta etiam crudelitatem unica qui incognita caussa sine consili sententiam plurumos sic capitis supplicio adfecisset neque externos tantummodo sed etiam centurionem civem Romanum crucifixisset qui militarem disciplinam ab divo Augusto institutam et servatam ab Tiberio Caesare Augusto corrupisset non solum indulgendo militibus <ne h>is qui ipsis praesunt more vetustissumo sic parerent sed etiam donativa suo nomine ex fisco principis nostri dando quo fact<o=I> milites alios Pisonianos alios Caesarianos dici laetatus sit honorando etiam eo<s> qui post <t>alis nominis usurpationem ipsi paruisse<n>t qui post mortem Germanici Caesaris quoius sic interitum <non> populus Romanus modo sed exterae quoque gentes luxserunt sic patri optumo sic et indulgentissumo sic libellum quo eum accusaret mittere ausus sit oblitus non tantum venerationis caritatisque quae principis filio debebantur ceterum humanitatis quoque quae ultra mortem <odia> non patiuntur procedere et cuius mortem gavisum esse eum <h>is argumentis senatu<i=S> apparauerit quod nefaria sacrificia ab eo facta quod naves quibus vehebantur <ornatae> sint quod recluser<i=VN>t deorum immortalium templa quae totius imperii Romani constantissuma sic pietas clauserat e<iu>sdemque habitus animi argumentum fuerit quod dedisse<t> congiarium ei qui nuntiaverit sibi de morte Germanici Caesaris probatum<q>(ue) sit frequenterq(ue) convivia habuisse eum iis ipsis diebus quibus de morte Germanici <Caesaris> ei nuntiatum erat numen quoque divi Augusti violatum esse ab eo arbitrari senatum omni honore qui aut memoriae eius aut imagin<ibu=E>s quae antequam in deorum numerum referretur ei redditae erant habebantur detracto quas ob res arbitrari senatum non optulisse sic eum se de<b=D>itae poenae sed maiori et quam inmin<e=O>rem sibi ab pietate et severitate iudicantium intellegebant subtraxisse itaque iis poenis quas a semet ipso exegisset adicere ne luctus mortis eius caussa sic a feminis quibus eis more <maiorum> si hoc senatus consultum factum non esset lugendus esset susciperetur utique statuae et imagines Cnaei Pisonis patris quae ubique positae essent tollerentur recte et ordine facturos qui quandoque familiae Calpurniae essent quive eam familiam cognatione adfinitateve contingerent si dedissent operam si quis eius genti<s> aut quis eorum qui cognatus adfinisve Calpurniae familiae fuisset mortuos esse<t> ne inter reliquas imagines <quae> exequias eorum funerum celebrare solent imago Cnaei Pisonis patris duceretur neve imaginibus <familiae> Calpurnia<e> imago eius interponeretur utique Cnaei Pisonis patris nomen tolleretur ex titulo statuae Germanici Caesari<s> quam ei sodales Augustales in campo ad aram Providentiae posuissent utique bona Cnaei Pisonis patris publicarenturE saltum placere Tiberio Caesari Augusto principi nostro cuius a patre divo Augusto Cnaeo Pisoni patri donatus erat reddi cum is idcirco dari eum sibi desideras<s>et quod <civitates> quarum <fines> hos saltus contingerent frequenter de iniuris Cnaei Pisonis patris libertorumque et servorum eius quaesita<e> esse<n>t a <t=D>que ob id providendum putaret ne postea iure meritoque soci populi Romani queri posse<nt> item senatum memorem clementiae suae iustitiae[que <atq>(ue) animi magnitudinis quas virtutes qu]as ab maioribus suis accepisset tum praecipue ab divo Augusto et Tiberio Caesare Augusto principibus suis didicisset ex bonis Cnaei Pisonis patris publicatis aequom sic humanumque censere filio eius Pisoni maiori de quo nihil esset dictum qui principis nostri quaestor fuisset et quem Germanicus quoque liberalitate sua honorasset qui complura modestiae suae posuisset pignora ex quibus sperari posset dissimillum<um> sic eum patri suo futurum donari nomine principis et senatus partem dimidiam bonorum eumque cum tanto beneficio obligaretur recte atque ordine facturum si praenomen patris mutasset Marco etiam Pisoni qu<o>i sic inpunitate<m> senatus humanitati et moderationi principis nostri adsensus dandam esset arbitraretur quo facilius inviolatum senatus beneficium ad eum perveniret alteram partem dimidiam bonorum paternorum dari ita ut ex omnibus bonis quae decreto senatus publicata et concessa eis essent nummum Xdecies centena milia dotis nomine Calpurniae Cnaei Pisonis filiae item peculi nomine nummum XXXXquadragies centena milia daretur item placere uti Cnaeus Piso pater supra portam Fontinalem quae inaedificasset iungendarum domum privatarum caussa! ea curatores locorum publicorum iudicandorum tollenda dimolienda curarent quod ad Plancinae caussam! pertineret quoi! plurum! et gravissuma sic crimina obiecta essent quoniam confiteretur se omnem spem in misericordia principis nostri et senatus habere et saepe princeps noster accurateque ab eo ordine petierit ut contentus senatus Cnaei Pisonis patris poena uxori eius sic uti Marco filio parceret et pro Plancina rogatu matris sua<e> depreca<tus> s<it> et quam ob rem id mater sua inpetrare vellet iustissuma<s> sic ab ea sibi causas expositas acceperit senatum arbitrari et Iuliae Augustae optime de re publica meritae non partu modo principis nostri sed etiam multis magnisque erga cuiusque ordinis homines beneficis quae cum iure meritoque plurumum! posse<t> in eo quod a senatu petere deberet parcissume sic uteretur eo et principis nostri summae erga matrem suam pietati suffragandum indulgendumque esse remittique poenam Plancinae placere Visellio Karo et Sempronio Basso comitibus Cnaei Pisonis patris et omnium malificiorum socis ac ministris aqua et igne interdici oportere ab eo praetore qui legem maiestatis quaereret bonaque eorum ab praetoribus qui aerario praeesse<n>t venire et in aerarium redigi placere item cum iudicaret senatus omnium partium pietatem antecess<iss>e Tiberium Caesarem Augustum principem nostrum tanti et tam aequalis dolori<s eius indicis> totiens conspectis quibus etiam senatus vehementer motus sit magnopere rogare et petere ut omnem curam quam in duos quondam filios suos partitus erat ad eum quem haberet converteret sperareque senatum eum qui supersit tanto maiori curae dis immortalibus fore quanto magis intellegerent omnem spem futuram paternae pro re publica stationis in uno repositam quo nomine debere eum finire dolorem ac restituere patriae suae non tantum animum sed etiam voltum qui publicae felicitati conveniret item senatum magnopere laudare Iuliae Augustae Drusique Caesaris moderationem imitantium principis nostri iustitiam quos animadverteret hunc ordinem non maiorem pietatem in Germanicum quam aequitatem in servandis integris iudicis suis donec de causa Cnaei Pisonis patris cognosceretur praestitisse ceterorum quoque contingentium Germanicum Caesarem necessitudine magnopere probare Agrippinae quam senatui memoriam divi Augusti quoi! fuisset probatissuma sic et viri Germanici cum quo unica concordia vixsisset et tot pignora edita partu felicissumo! eorum qui superessent commendare itemque Antoniae Germanici Caesaris matris quae unum matrimonium Drusi Germanici patris experta sanctitate morum dignam se divo Augusto tam arta propinquitate exhibuerit et Liviae sororis Germanici Caesaris de qua optume sic et avia sua et socer idemque patruos princeps noster iudicaret quorum iudicis etiam si non contingeret domum eorum merito gloriari posset nedum tam coniunctis necessitudinibus inligata femina quarum aeque et dolorem fidelissumum sic et in dolore moderatione<m> senatum probare item quod filiorum Germanici puerilis et praecipue in Neronem Caesarem iam etiam [iuvenis dolor amisso patre tali itemque Tiberi Germanici fratris Ti(beri) Germanici Caesaris non e]xc<e=V>sserit modum probabilem iudicare senatum referendum quidem esse acceptum maxume! discipulinae avi eorum et patrui et Iuliae Augustae sed tam<en> ipsorum quoque nomine laudandum existumaret item equestris ordinis curam et industriam unic<e=I> senatui probari quod fideliter intellexsisset quanta res et quam ad omnium salutem pietatemque pertinens ageretur et quod frequentibus adclamationibus adfectum animi sui et dolorem de principis nostri filique eius iniuris ac pro rei publicae utilitate testatus sit plebem quoque laudare senatum quod cum equestri ordine consenserit pietatemque suam erga principem nostrum memoriamque fili eius significaverit et cum effusissumis! studis ad repraesentandam <poenam> Cnaei Pisonis patris ab semet ipsa accensa esset regi tamen exemplo equestris ordinis a principe nostro <se passa> sit item senatum probare eorum militum fidem quorum animi frustra sollicitati essent scelere Cnaei Pisonis patris omnesque qui <sub> auspicis et imperio principis nostri milites essent quam fidem pietatemque domui Augustae praestarent eam sperare perpetuo praestaturos cum scirent salutem imperi nostri in eius domus custodia positam esse senatum arbitrari eorum curae atque offici esse ut aput! eos ii qui quandoque eis praeessent plurumum! auctoritatis haberent qui fidelissuma sic pietate salutare huic urbi imperioque populi Romani nomen Caesarum coluissent et quo facilius totius actae rei ordo posterorum memoriae tradi posset atque iis scirent quid et de singulari moderatione Germanici Caesaris et de sceleribus Cnaei Pisonis patris senatus iudicasset placere uti oratio quam recitasset princeps noster itemque haec senatus consulta in aere incisa quo loco Tiberio Caesari Augusto videretur ponere<n>tur utique hoc senatus consultum in cuiusque provinciae celeberruma! urbe eiusque in urbis ipsius celeberrumo! loco in aere incisum figerentur itemque hoc senatus consultum in hibernis cuiusque legionis at signa figeretur censuerunt in senatu fuerunt CCCI hoc senatus consultum factum est per relationem solum Tiberius Caesar Augustus tribunicia potestate XXII manu mea scripsi velle me hoc senatus consultum quod est factum IIII Idus Decembres Cotta et Messa<l>la consulibus referente me scriptum referri in tabulas publicas \n
EDCS-46400006.translation: On the fourth day before the Ides of December, in the Palace, in the portico by the Temple of Apollo, the following were present for the recording: Marcus Valerius Messallinus, son of Marcus, of the Lemonia tribe; Gaius Ateius Capito, son of Lucius, of the Aniensis tribe; Sextus Pompeius Priscus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensis tribe; Marcus Pompeius Priscus, son of Marcus, of the Teretina tribe; Gaius Arrenus Gallus, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe; Lucius Nonius Arenas, son of Lucius, of the Pomptina tribe, quaestor. Marcus Vinicius, son of Publius, of the Poblilia tribe, quaestor. Because Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding the tribunician power for the 22nd time, consul for the 3rd time, designated for the 4th, reported to the Senate concerning the case of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and whether he seemed to have committed suicide justly, and concerning the case of Marcus Piso, and added a request that the order remember his plea for the young man, and concerning the case of Plancina, for whom he had pleaded and for which reasons he had previously explained, and what the Senate should judge concerning Visellius Karo and Sempronius Bassus, companions of Gnaeus Piso, the elder. The Senate therefore decreed as follows: That the Senate and the Roman People should give thanks above all to the immortal gods, because through the impious schemes of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, they did not suffer the tranquility of the present state of the Republic, which could not be better, to be disturbed, and that we enjoy the benevolence of our princeps. Then, thanks to Tiberius Caesar Augustus, our princeps, for providing the Senate with the means for all matters necessary to ascertain the truth, whose fairness and patience the Senate admired, also for this reason: that although the crimes of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, were manifest, and he himself had taken his own life, nevertheless he wished his case to be investigated, and had summoned his sons, encouraged them to defend their father's case, and even wished him, who was not yet of senatorial rank, to be introduced into the Senate, and granted both parties the opportunity to speak for the father and for themselves, and for Marcus Piso. And so, although the case has been debated for some days, and letters written by the accusers of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and by Gnaeus Piso himself, have been read, and exemplars of the codicils which Germanicus Caesar had written to Gnaeus Piso, the elder, have been read, and witnesses have been produced, and considering the rank of each of them, the Senate is amazed at the singular moderation and patience of Germanicus Caesar, which was overcome by the ferocity of the character of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and for this reason, that the dying Germanicus Caesar, who himself testified that Gnaeus Piso, the elder, was the cause of his death, not without reason renounced his friendship, and that he, who should have remembered that he was given as an assistant to Germanicus Caesar, who had been sent by our princeps, with the authority of this order, to compose the state of overseas territories, desiring the presence of either Tiberius Caesar Augustus himself or one of his sons, and having neglected the majesty of the Augustan house, and also the public law that, as proconsul, and concerning him, a law had been passed to the people, that in whatever province he came, his command would be greater than that of him who held that province as proconsul, as long as in all things his command was greater than Germanicus Caesar's, as if all things should be subject to his will and power, he nevertheless acted in the province of Syria, having stirred up war with Armenia and Parthia as much as was in him, because he did not want to remove Vonones, who was suspected by the King of the Parthians, further from his custody, as he had done according to the commands of our princeps and the frequent letters of Germanicus Caesar when he was absent, and because he allowed certain Armenians, wicked and audacious, to converse with Vonones, so that through them he might stir up unrest in Armenia, and that Vonones, either having killed or expelled the king of Armenia, whom Germanicus Caesar, with the consent of his father and the Senate, had given to that nation as king, should seize it, and having corrupted by great gifts of Vonones, he attempted to stir up civil war. Long ago, by the divine will of the deified Augustus and the virtues of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, all civil wars having been buried, he sought to reclaim the province of Syria after the death of Germanicus Caesar, which he had left behind when he was alive, with the worst spirit and example, and for this reason the soldiers of Rome attempted to clash with each other. He also, it was perceived, had cruelly executed many without trial, not only foreigners but also a centurion, a Roman citizen, whom he had crucified, who had corrupted the military discipline instituted by the deified Augustus and preserved by Tiberius Caesar Augustus, not only by indulging the soldiers against those who are over them, in the most ancient manner of obedience, but also by giving donatives in his own name from the treasury of our princeps, rejoicing that soldiers were called either Piso's or Caesar's, and even by honoring those who obeyed him after the usurpation of that name, who, after the death of Germanicus Caesar, whose demise not only the Roman People but also foreign nations mourned, dared to send a letter accusing him, forgetting not only the reverence and affection due to the princeps' son, but also humanity, which does not allow hatred to extend beyond death, and whose death he seemed to have rejoiced in, as the Senate had gathered from the following evidence: that impious sacrifices had been made by him, that the ships on which they sailed were adorned, that he had reopened the temples of the immortal gods, which the most constant piety of the entire Roman Empire had closed, and that the same disposition of mind was evidenced by the fact that he gave a gift to him who brought him the news of the death of Germanicus Caesar, and that he held frequent banquets on those very days on which he was informed of the death of Germanicus Caesar. The Senate also considers that the divine will of the deified Augustus was violated by him, in that all honor which was paid to his memory or to his images, before he was numbered among the gods, was removed. For these reasons, the Senate considers that he did not offer himself to the penalty he deserved, but rather removed himself from a greater one, which the piety and severity of those judging him understood to be impending. And so, to the penalties which he exacted from himself, it was added that the mourning for his death should not be undertaken by women, for whom, by the custom of our ancestors, it would have been mournful if this decree of the Senate had not been made, and that the statues and images of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, wherever they were placed, should be removed. Those who belonged to the Calpurnian family or were related to it by kinship or affinity would do rightly and in order if they took care that the image of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, should not be carried among the other images that celebrate the funerals of their dead, nor should his image be placed among the images of the Calpurnian family, and that the name of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, should be removed from the inscription of the statue of Germanicus Caesar, which the sodales Augustales had placed for him in the Campus near the altar of Providentia. And that the property of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, should be confiscated. It is also decreed that the saltus (estate) which was given to Tiberius Caesar Augustus, our princeps, by his father, the deified Augustus, should be returned to him, because he desired it to be given to him for this reason: that the civitates whose borders touched these estates had frequently complained of the injustices of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and his freedmen and slaves, and therefore it was thought necessary to provide that they would not be able to complain justly and deservedly afterwards. Likewise, the Senate, mindful of its clemency, justice, and greatness of spirit, virtues which it had received from its ancestors, and particularly from the deified Augustus and Tiberius Caesar Augustus, our princes, decrees that it is fair and humane, from the confiscated property of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, to grant to his son Piso, the elder, concerning whom nothing was said, who had been quaestor of our princeps and whom Germanicus had honored with his generosity, and who had given many pledges of his modesty, from which it could be hoped that he would be very unlike his father, half of the property in the name of the princeps and the Senate. And binding him with such a great favor, he would act rightly and in order if he changed his father's praenomen. To Marcus Piso also, to whom the Senate, assenting to the humanity and moderation of our princeps, decided that impunity should be granted, so that the inviolable benefit of the Senate might more easily reach him, the other half of his paternal property should be given, in such a way that from all the property confiscated and granted to them by decree of the Senate, ten million sesterces should be given as dowry in the name of Calpurnia, daughter of Gnaeus Piso, and forty million sesterces as personal property. It is also decreed that the curators of public lands and buildings should be ordered to remove and demolish what Gnaeus Piso, the elder, had built above the Porta Fontinalis for the purpose of joining private houses, as it pertained to the case of Plancina, against whom many and very serious charges had been brought, since she admitted that she had placed all hope in the mercy of our princeps and the Senate, and she had often and carefully asked this order that the Senate be content with the penalty of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and spare his wife and son Marcus, and she had pleaded for Plancina at the request of her mother, and for this reason her mother wished to obtain it, and she had explained the most just reasons to her. Therefore, the Senate considers that it should support the supreme piety of our princeps towards his mother, and indulge her, and that the penalty for Plancina should be remitted. It is decreed that Visellius Karo and Sempronius Bassus, companions of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and accomplices and ministers of all wicked deeds, should be interdicted from fire and water by the praetor who inquires into the law of treason, and that their property should be confiscated by the praetors who preside over the treasury and brought into the treasury. It is also decreed that when the Senate judged, the piety of all parties had preceded Tiberius Caesar Augustus, our princeps, in such great and equal grief, so often witnessed, by which the Senate itself was vehemently moved, it greatly begged and requested that he turn all the care which he had divided between his two former sons towards the one he had, and hope that he would be of even greater concern to the immortal gods than he was, the more they understood that all hope for his future service to the Republic rested on him alone, by which name he should end his grief and restore to his country not only his spirit but also his countenance, which would be fitting for public happiness. Likewise, the Senate greatly praises the moderation of Julia Augusta and Drusus Caesar, imitating our princeps' justice, and observes that this order showed no greater piety towards Germanicus than fairness in maintaining its judgments until the case of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, was heard. It also greatly approves of the loyalty of Agrippina, who was related to Germanicus Caesar, to the Senate, remembering the deified Augustus, to whom she had been most pleasing, and to her husband Germanicus, with whom she had lived in unique harmony, and the many pledges born from their most fortunate union, of those who survived, and also of Antonia, mother of Germanicus Caesar, who, having experienced only one marriage with Drusus Germanicus, had shown herself worthy of the deified Augustus through the sanctity of her morals, by such close kinship, and also of Livia, sister of Germanicus Caesar, concerning whom our princeps, her grandmother and father-in-law, and uncle, judged very well, and whose judgments, even if they were not related to their household, could rightly be praised, let alone a woman bound by such close relationships, whose grief, both most faithful and moderate, the Senate approves. Likewise, it is considered probable that the grief of the sons of Germanicus, especially Nero Caesar, now already a young man, for the loss of such a father, and also Tiberius Germanicus, brother of Tiberius Germanicus Caesar, has not exceeded the probable limit. The Senate judges that it should indeed be referred to, having been most acceptably received by the discipline of their grandfather and uncle and Julia Augusta, but nevertheless, it judges that it should be praised in their names as well. Likewise, the Senate greatly approves of the care and industry of the equestrian order, for having faithfully understood how important and relevant to the safety and piety of all was the matter being handled, and for having testified through frequent acclamations their state of mind and grief for the injuries to our princeps and his son, and for the good of the Republic. It also praises the plebs, for having agreed with the equestrian order and having shown its piety towards our princeps and the memory of his son, and for having allowed themselves to be inflamed with the most fervent zeal for the punishment of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, by the example of the equestrian order, and by our princeps. Likewise, the Senate approves the loyalty of those soldiers whose minds were in vain agitated by the wickedness of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, and that all who are soldiers under the auspices and command of our princeps would perpetually show the loyalty and piety they owe to the Augustan house, knowing that the safety of the empire rests on the custody of that house. The Senate considers it their duty and office to ensure that those who will command them in the future have the greatest authority, who have cultivated the name of Caesar with the most faithful piety towards this city and the empire of the Roman People, and so that the order of all that has been done can be more easily handed down to the memory of posterity, and so that they may know what the Senate judged concerning the singular moderation of Germanicus Caesar and the crimes of Gnaeus Piso, the elder, it is decreed that the speech which our princeps recited, and also these decrees of the Senate, should be inscribed on bronze in whatever place Tiberius Caesar Augustus deems fit to place them, and that this decree of the Senate should be inscribed on bronze and posted in the most frequented city of each province and in the most frequented place of that city, and also in the winter quarters of each legion and posted at the standards. They decreed that there were 301 senators present. This decree of the Senate was made by the sole report of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, holding the tribunician power for the 22nd time. I wrote with my own hand that I wish this decree of the Senate, which was made on the fourth day before the Ides of December, with Cotta and Messalla as consuls, upon my report, to be written and entered into the public records.
EDCS-46400006.summary: This is a lengthy senatorial decree from AD 20, detailing the trial and condemnation of Gnaeus Piso for treason and the murder of Germanicus Caesar, outlining punishments for his associates and decisions regarding his family and property, and commending the loyalty of various groups. This decree was issued in the consulship of Cotta and Messalla, under Tiberius Caesar Augustus.

EDCS-46600203.translation: Kresson, to you I bring immortal gifts of gold and silver, most excellent Artemis and Thera, far superior to the Muses. And it is not fitting to bring gifts of others' possessions to a god. Arrianus, proconsul.
EDCS-46600203.summary: A votive inscription to Artemis and Thera, likely from the 2nd century AD, by Arrianus, a proconsul, stating that these gifts of gold and silver are superior to the Muses and belong to the goddess, not to others.

EDCS-49300085.translation: Tuccudus, aged 18 years. His mother took care to have (this made).
EDCS-49300085.summary: A tombstone for Tuccudus, aged 18, erected by his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400906.translation: Wife, aged 50 years, lies here. This work was begun by Gaius Servilius Severus.
EDCS-51400906.summary: A tombstone for a wife aged 50, indicating the work was started by Gaius Servilius Severus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-54900655.translation: Let Marcellus Valerius be mute and silent towards Gaius Licinius Gallus, just as a frog is mute and silent without a tongue, so let Marcellus be mute and silent and debilitated towards Licinius Gallus.
EDCS-54900655.summary: An inscription expressing a curse or imprecation against Gaius Licinius Gallus, enacted by Marcellus Valerius, possibly from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-58800017.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Ceplita Maria lived 60 years. Her freedman and heir Moeilus took care to have this made for his mother. She lies here. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-58800017.summary: A tombstone for Ceplita Maria, who lived 60 years, erected by her freedman and heir Moeilus, likely from the Roman Imperial period, with a common epitaph.

EDCS-60300291.translation: Marcus Iunius Marcellus, son of Marcus of the Galeria tribe, built the forum of Augustus at his own expense. He served as a duovir.
EDCS-60300291.summary: This inscription records the construction of the forum of Augustus by Marcus Iunius Marcellus, who also held the office of duovir, likely in the 1st century CE.

EDCS-60300321.translation: The property of Quintus Publicius Placidus, 24 feet long.
EDCS-60300321.summary: This inscription likely marks the dimensions of a property belonging to Quintus Publicius Placidus, possibly on a tomb or boundary stone.

EDCS-64200002.translation: Ampliata, dear to her family, lived 21 years. She lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-64200002.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ampliata, who died at age 21, with a common Latin wish for peace in the afterlife.

EDCS-64600434.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pelagia, 25 years old, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-64600434.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pelagia, who died at age 25, with a dedication to the Manes and a wish for a peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-64900628.translation: ... 70 years old, dutiful to her family, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-64900628.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph for a woman who died at age 70, emphasizing her devotion to her family and wishing her peace in death.

EDCS-64900630.translation: Gaius Caecilius, son of Gaius, freedman, lived 50 years. He lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. Frontage 26 feet, depth 15 feet.
EDCS-64900630.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Caecilius, a freedman, who died at age 50, also specifying the dimensions of his burial plot.

EDCS-64900632.translation: ... dutiful(?) to his family, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-64900632.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph, likely for a male, expressing a wish for peace in the afterlife and indicating familial piety.

EDCS-65000077.translation: To the Genius of the citizens of the municipality of Regina. Lucius Calpurnius Mercator gave and dedicated this gem, in fulfillment of a vow, at his own expense.
EDCS-65000077.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication of a gem by Lucius Calpurnius Mercator to the Genius of the municipality of Regina.

EDCS-65000122.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Iulio Constantio nobilissimo Caesari Imperatori Caesari Constan \n
EDCS-65000122.translation: To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, most noble Caesar, Emperor Caesar...
EDCS-65000122.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription honoring the Caesar Flavius Julius Constantius, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-66300069.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Bruttia Prisca lived 39 years.
EDCS-66300069.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bruttia Prisca, who died at the age of 39, with a dedication to the Manes.

EDCS-66300085.translation: Cocceia Severa from Norba, aged 80, priestess of the province of Baetica and of the people of Norba, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-66300085.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Cocceia Severa, a priestess, who died at the age of 80.

EDCS-67400283.summary: This is a dedication by Iunia Avita, likely to a deity or for a public building.

EDCS-67400288.translation: Of the two Junia juniors, Melissus and Melissa.
EDCS-67400288.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, possibly siblings or a couple, perhaps associated with a guild or a monument.

EDCS-67400289.summary: This inscription refers to potters named Gemelliani, possibly indicating a workshop or a group of artisans.

EDCS-68200001.translation: Marcus Gavius Amphio, son of Marcus and Lucius, freedman, precinct master, dedicated the precinct of the Veneri precinct and a portico at his own expense.
EDCS-68200001.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a precinct and a portico by Marcus Gavius Amphio, a precinct master, at his own expense.

EDCS-68300039.translation: Sextus Licinius Satullius, son of Sextus and freedman. Licinia Alauca, daughter of Sextus and freedwoman, mother. Licinia Philema, daughter of Sextus and freedwoman, wife.
EDCS-68300039.summary: This inscription lists the names and relationships of three individuals: Sextus Licinius Satullius, his mother Licinia Alauca, and his wife Licinia Philema.

EDCS-68600135.translation: Philologus and Publius Aelius... of the citizens of the municipality... gave at their own expense.
EDCS-68600135.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication made at their own expense by Philologus and Publius Aelius for the citizens of a municipality.

EDCS-69200322.translation: Severus Gluantiocus arranged for this to be made at his own expense.
EDCS-69200322.summary: This inscription states that Severus Gluantiocus arranged for the construction or erection of this monument at his own expense.

EDCS-69200596.translation: To Marcus Fabius, son of Gnaeus, of the Galeria tribe, Attilius Antonio Avitus, duovir.
EDCS-69200596.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Fabius (of the Galeria tribe) by Attilius Antonio Avitus, who held the office of duovir.

EDCS-70700054.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely serving as a filiation, indicating the father of an individual.

EDCS-70700056.summary: This is a personal name, likely a property owner or individual associated with Sextus Avillus, possibly from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-70700058.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or origin from a workshop belonging to an individual named Primus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700080.translation: To Tiberius Aelius, son of Quintus, grandson of Sextus.
EDCS-70700080.summary: This is a fragmentary personal dedication for Tiberius Aelius, son of Quintus and grandson of Sextus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700082.translation: Lucius Alfidius Ingenuus, son of Lucius, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-70700082.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Alfidius Ingenuus, recording his burial and a wish for his repose, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71100088.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Gaius Vividius Valerianus, son of Gaius, of the Valeria tribe, 11 years old, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-71100088.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Vividius Valerianus, a young boy who died at age 11, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300075.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Vinuleia Severa, aged 1 year and 6 months. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-71300075.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vinuleia Severa, a young child, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300104.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Cornelia Modestina, 18 years old, dutiful to her family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-71300104.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Modestina, a young woman who died at 18, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300338.translation: To Quintus Fabius Rusticus, son of Quintus, grandson of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, husband of Valerius Cinna Lucian, former prefect of artisans in Rome, twice chief magistrate, priest of Augustus. Fabia Quintilliana placed the base and statue from her will.
EDCS-71300338.summary: This is a dedication for Quintus Fabius Rusticus, a prominent Roman official and priest, by his wife Fabia Quintilliana, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300342.translation: ...at the expense of the most deserving youth and patron.
EDCS-71300342.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a benefactor who funded a project or memorial for a young patron, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200001.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription possibly for Aemilia, with Marcus Aemilius mentioned as a relative or dedicator, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72600014.translation: Lucius Cornelius Campanus, son of Quintus, priest of Salus, made this at his own expense again.
EDCS-72600014.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Lucius Cornelius Campanus, a priest of Salus, at his own expense.

EDCS-72600020.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Cornelius Daphnus, freedman of Quintus, of Patricium, aged 55, pious to his own, lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-72600020.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Cornelius Daphnus, a freedman aged 55, who is described as pious and lies buried here.

EDCS-72600030.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Caecilianus lies here.
EDCS-72600030.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius Caecilianus, recording his burial.

EDCS-72600049.translation: Titus Statilius Superatus of Astigi, [official/member] of the colony Augusta Firma, dedicated [a statue of] Mercury Augustus (?) at his own expense.
EDCS-72600049.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue, possibly of Mercury Augustus, by Titus Statilius Superatus for the colony Augusta Firma, funded by himself.

EDCS-72800099.translation: Lucius Julius Rex set up this offering in fulfillment of a vow to Fontanus and Fontilia.
EDCS-72800099.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Lucius Julius Rex to the deities Fontanus and Fontilia.

EDCS-72800102.translation: [This is the tomb of] Valerius Maximus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, a veteran from the Classica legion, lies here.
EDCS-72800102.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Valerius Maximus, a veteran of the Classica legion.

EDCS-72800130.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Restituta, aged 40, lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-72800130.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Restituta, aged 40, recording her burial.

EDCS-73900025.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cornelia Rufina, daughter of Lucius, repaid the expense for her most pious uncle Marcus Valerius Flaccus, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-73900025.summary: This inscription records Cornelia Rufina repaying expenses for her uncle, Marcus Valerius Flaccus, a gesture of piety.

EDCS-73900029.translation: For Didia Parthenope, aged 5. Her most unhappy father DVN ARS SV dedicated this to his incomparable daughter; she lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-73900029.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Didia Parthenope, aged 5, dedicated by her grieving father.

EDCS-74200418.text_edition: \n Cornelia Luci liberta Sphragis annorum XX hic sita est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-74200418.translation: Cornelia Sphragis, freedwoman of Lucius, aged 20, lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-74200418.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Cornelia Sphragis, a freedwoman aged 20.

EDCS-74200617.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Luna Augusta, likely on a shrine or altar, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-74700515.translation: To Augusta Victoria, by order of Mars.
EDCS-74700515.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a dedication to the goddess Victoria Augusta, possibly commissioned by someone related to Mars, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300082.translation: To Julianus, aged [1] years, he is placed here; may the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-75300082.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young person named Julianus, who died at the age of 1, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300084.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Aurelia Patricia(?) lived 11 years; she is placed here; may the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-75300084.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Patricia, who died at the age of 11, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300085.translation: To Veri[---], patroness, well deserved, to the good, well.
EDCS-75300085.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication to a female patron named Veri[---] for her good deeds, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300086.translation: From Crocale, freedwoman of Lucius, aged 22 years.
EDCS-75300086.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Crocale, a freedwoman of Lucius, who died at the age of 22, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300095.translation: Husband [---] son of Publius, of the voting tribe Quirina, [---]us made [it].
EDCS-75300095.summary: This fragmented inscription records a husband, identified by his father and tribal affiliation, who erected a monument, likely a tomb, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300183.summary: This inscription records that Emperor Hadrian built something, likely a public work or building, dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-75600219.translation: Lucius Aelius Aelianus, duovir, of the citizens of the municipium Flavium Naevensium, with Egnatia Lupercilla, daughter of Marcus, his wife, by providing additional roof tiles and awnings, gave a feast to the citizens and inhabitants of both sexes.
EDCS-75600219.summary: This inscription records a public benefaction by Lucius Aelius Aelianus and his wife Egnatia Lupercilla, including providing building materials and hosting a feast for the citizens of Naevis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75800055.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Trajan, likely commemorating his victories in Germania, dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-75900041.translation: Lucius Postumius Optatus, having freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow, made the pavement.
EDCS-75900041.summary: This dedication records Lucius Postumius Optatus fulfilling a vow by dedicating a pavement, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-75900042.translation: Gnaeus Pompeius Claruss, 25 years old, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75900042.summary: This is a tombstone for Gnaeus Pompeius Clarus, who died at age 25, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900211.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Paula Fabia, who lived 30 or 40 years, 6 months, and 5 days; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75900211.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Paula Fabia, who lived approximately 30-40 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900222.translation: Nothus, 30 years old, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75900222.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Nothus, who died at age 30, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900224.translation: Most unhappy (made this) for his most devoted daughter.
EDCS-75900224.summary: This funerary monument was set up by a grieving parent for their beloved daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900229.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a funerary text, mentioning an age of 85 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900231.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Nicias, who lived 60 years, a comrade.
EDCS-75900231.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Nicias, who lived 60 years and was a comrade, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900232.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aelia Marrucina, who lived 38 years, dutiful to her family, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-75900232.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aelia Marrucina at age 38, who is described as dutiful, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75900234.translation: [...] for the most deserving and young patron.
EDCS-75900234.summary: This dedication honors a highly deserving young patron, possibly a funerary monument or a dedication of a public work, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-76900064.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Nomion.
EDCS-76900064.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Nomion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-77400002.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Severus Pius.
EDCS-77400002.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial dedication, likely to Caracalla, mentioning his imperial titles and parentage.

EDCS-78200090.translation: Marcus Calpurnius, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe, concerning the [----] and the baths, [----] near the bath [----] for the protection [----].
EDCS-78200090.summary: This inscription records the restoration or construction of baths and a related structure by Marcus Calpurnius, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-80400012.translation: To the Emperor Flavius Valerius Maximinius, the most noble Caesar.
EDCS-80400012.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Flavius Valerius Maximinius (also known as Maximinus Daia), likely set up during his reign.

EDCS-80400017.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius [D]omitius Aurelianus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul, restored [this].
EDCS-80400017.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by Emperor Aurelian, detailing his imperial titles and attributes.

EDCS-80400019.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription likely intended to honor or record an action by an emperor, possibly Maximian.

EDCS-80900213.translation: To C. C. V. and L. I. V., more pleasing.
EDCS-80900213.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that seems to be a dedication, possibly to deities or individuals, from an unknown period.

EDCS-80900293.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Mamilius Chrestus, aged 72 years, is buried here. I beg you, passerby, as you go, to say: 'May the earth be light for you.'
EDCS-80900293.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Mamilius Chrestus, recording his name, age, burial location, and a common plea for passersby, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200158.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely the beginning of a dedication or epitaph mentioning Tiberius Calpurnius, from an unknown period.

EDCS-81200159.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Remusa, aged 52 years, beloved by her family, is buried here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-81200159.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Remusa, recording her name, age, burial, and a common plea for passersby, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200165.translation: To Auge, Glycera, Sabina, Fortunata, Nicoclis, Prima, Nicerotis, Macedonis, Ingenui, Galla, Philarguri, Dionusi, Chiteridis, Niconis, Augenis, Chrestus, Maritumi, Basili, Mageia.
EDCS-81200165.summary: This is a list of names, possibly a dedication or a record of individuals associated with a cult or a communal event, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81200166.translation: May Primus be silent, so that all may be silent; silent, silent, [---]. Stephanu[s]. Primus. Auctus. Witnesses. SAET[---]. S[---]. [---]us lies silent. Let [---] lie silent, unmarked. Let [---] lie breathless. [---]us. Let [---] lie breathless, a silent [---]. [---]us, silent. [---] silent.
EDCS-81200166.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription seems to be a curse or warning against disturbing the dead, with repeated emphasis on silence and breathlessness, likely dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-81200167.translation: Auctus. Iuvenis Placidus. Senecio Marinus. Faustus Chius, freedman of Annaeus, and his household. Oceanus. Castor Secundus.
EDCS-81200167.summary: This is a list of names, likely of individuals associated with a household or a group, with the inscription dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81200169.summary: This is a very brief inscription consisting of initials, possibly a name or ownership mark, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-81200179.translation: Decimus V. B. erected [this] for Nabia.
EDCS-81200179.summary: This is a funerary or dedication inscription by Decimus V. B. for a person named Nabia.

EDCS-81800074.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Telesinus, aged seventeen, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-81800074.summary: This is a standard funerary epitaph for Telesinus, a seventeen-year-old, indicating his burial place and including a common wish for the deceased, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800076.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning a freedman of Marcus named Laetus and another individual named Lepida, likely dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800077.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription mentioning a possible freedman and a deceased person aged twenty-eight, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800078.translation: Acuto, aged six, son of Titus.
EDCS-81800078.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Acuto, the six-year-old son of Titus, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800080.translation: [---] to Dorothea, freedwoman of Marcus, daughter of Atia. Fannia, freedwoman of Fannius, [had this] made.
EDCS-81800080.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by Fannia, a freedwoman, for Dorothea, another freedwoman, likely dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800082.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the names of individuals, possibly in a funerary context, with the dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-81800083.translation: To Daphne, Calliope, the pious mother, Fannia Capratina, freedwoman of Caius.
EDCS-81800083.summary: This inscription is a dedication to deities, likely on a tomb or altar, set up by Fannia Capratina.

EDCS-81800084.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Turranius Primus, aged 2 years and 2 months, pious to his family, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-81800084.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Turranius Primus, a child.

EDCS-81800089.translation: Acutus willingly paid his vow and gave the gift.
EDCS-81800089.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Acutus.

EDCS-81800094.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of the deified Augustus, great-great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar, holding tribunician power, emperor, consul...
EDCS-81800094.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial inscription detailing the lineage and titles of Emperor Nero Claudius.

EDCS-81800097.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Severus Pius, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius...
EDCS-81800097.summary: This is a fragment of an imperial inscription detailing the lineage of an emperor, likely Caracalla.

EDCS-82800056.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Carpophorus, aged 20 years, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. Calliope, mother, Tom[---], father.
EDCS-82800056.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Carpophorus, with his parents Calliope and Tom[---] mentioned.

EDCS-00380402.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Rocius.
EDCS-00380402.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Gaius Rocius to the Dis Manes, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-03000494.translation: Hispanus of the Quirina tribe, aged 6 years, dutiful to his own, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. The town council of Vergium decreed a burial place and funeral expenses for him.
EDCS-03000494.summary: This tombstone records the burial of a young boy, Hispanus, and details the provision of burial expenses by the town council of Vergium, suggesting a posthumous honor.

EDCS-03300376.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caio Baebio Masculo from Turubrigia, aged 17 years, 6 months, and 29 days, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300376.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caio Baebio Masculo, a young man from Turubrigia, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-03300377.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Aurelia Annita, daughter of Arconis, aged 16 years, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300377.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Aurelia Annita, daughter of Arconis, who died at the age of 16.

EDCS-03300418.translation: Veneriosus, a servant of God, lived 65 years, 3 months, and 20 days. He departed in peace on the eighth day before the Ides of January in the year 535 (of the era).
EDCS-03300418.summary: This epitaph records the death of Veneriosus, a Christian servant, including his age and the date of his passing according to a local era.

EDCS-03300419.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Ovius Fl., aged 50 years, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300419.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Marcus Ovius Fl., aged 50, to the Dis Manes.

EDCS-03300437.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Iunius Licinius Ingenuus, aged 15 years, dear to his own, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300437.summary: This tombstone is a funerary dedication for Quintus Iunius Licinius Ingenuus, a 15-year-old boy who was beloved by his family.

EDCS-03300438.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. The girl Pompeia Clara lived 3 years, dutiful to her own, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300438.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of a young girl named Pompeia Clara, who died at the age of 3.

EDCS-03300442.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a dedication or funerary marker, mentioning the son of someone named Quinti.

EDCS-03300452.summary: This is a simple name inscription, possibly identifying an individual on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-03300454.translation: May the earth lie lightly upon Pis, your wife.
EDCS-03300454.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Pis, expressing a wish for her peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300458.translation: Quintus Iunius Sodalis is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300458.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Iunius Sodalis, stating his burial and expressing a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300459.translation: Lucius Valerius Annius Valerianus lived 2 years, 4 months; dear to his own, he is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300459.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Lucius Valerius Annius Valerianus, who died as a child, and expresses a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300460.translation: [...]ria Calma, dear to her own, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you. [---]us Herculanus is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300460.summary: This inscription appears to be funerary, possibly recording two burials, with wishes for peaceful rest for both individuals.

EDCS-03300461.translation: Sacred to the Deities of the Underworld. Lucius Iulius Philinus, dear to his own, 33 years old, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300461.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Deities of the Underworld for Lucius Iulius Philinus, who died at age 33, and expresses a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300462.translation: Marcus Valerius Optate, 6 years old, dear to his own.
EDCS-03300462.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Valerius Optate at the age of six, noting he was dear to his family.

EDCS-03300465.translation: Baebia Cai filia Ciniamilla, dear to her own, 15 years old.
EDCS-03300465.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Baebia Cai filia Ciniamilla at the age of fifteen, noting she was dear to her family.

EDCS-03300466.translation: Iulia Firmina, dear to her own, 60 years old, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300466.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Firmina, who died at age 60, stating her burial and expressing a wish for her peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300467.translation: Lucius Herennius Herculaneus, dear to his own, 60 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-03300467.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lucius Herennius Herculaneus, who died at age 60, noting he was dear to his family.

EDCS-03300468.translation: Iulius Saturninus, 80 years old, is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300468.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius Saturninus, who died at age 80, stating his burial and expressing a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-03300469.translation: Iucunda, dear to her family, aged 33.
EDCS-03300469.summary: This inscription records the age and familial affection for a woman named Iucunda.

EDCS-03300471.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Rius Capel, aged 51, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300471.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rius Capel, who died at the age of 51, including a common epitaph wishing him peace in the afterlife.

EDCS-03300475.translation: Lucius Cornelius Aiax is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300475.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Cornelius Aiax, including a standard epitaph.

EDCS-03300479.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods. Pompeius Tisinauca, aged 15. His mother, Pompeia Turpilla, had this made for her most devoted son. He is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300479.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Pompeius Tisinauca at age 15, commissioned by his devoted mother, Pompeia Turpilla.

EDCS-03300482.translation: Caius Valerius Primus is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300482.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Valerius Primus, including a standard epitaph.

EDCS-03300483.translation: Successa is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300483.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Successa, including a standard epitaph.

EDCS-03300484.translation: Aelia Opsequens, dear to her family, is buried here.
EDCS-03300484.summary: This inscription records that Aelia Opsequens, who was dear to her family, is buried here.

EDCS-03300485.translation: Marcus Antonius Teuda, aged 90. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300485.summary: This inscription records the age of Marcus Antonius Teuda, who died at 90.

EDCS-03300486.translation: Anthusa, freedwoman of Athenodorus and Caius, aged 40, dear to her family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300486.summary: This funerary inscription records that Anthusa, a freedwoman aged 40, beloved by her family, is buried here.

EDCS-03300487.translation: Lucius Caecilius Romulus, aged 75, dear to his family, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-03300487.summary: This funerary inscription records that Lucius Caecilius Romulus, aged 75 and dear to his family, is buried here.

EDCS-03300488.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Fortunatus, aged 50. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300488.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fortunatus, aged 50.

EDCS-03300489.translation: Primitivus, aged 25, dear to his own people, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300489.summary: A funerary epitaph for Primitivus, aged 25.

EDCS-03300490.translation: Herennius, aged 20, dear to his own people, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-03300490.summary: A funerary epitaph for Herennius, aged 20.

EDCS-03300492.summary: An dedication to the god Hercules of Gades.

EDCS-03300493.translation: Lucius Manilius made this, son of Marcius.
EDCS-03300493.summary: An inscription recording a maker, Lucius Manilius, son of Marcius.

EDCS-04900508.translation: From Ilia Ilipa Agria Ianuaria, priestess of Ilipa, to Augustus Silvanus.
EDCS-04900508.summary: A dedication by Agria Ianuaria, priestess of Ilipa, to Augustus Silvanus.

EDCS-04900509.translation: He paid willingly and deservedly from a vow.
EDCS-04900509.summary: A record of fulfilling a vow, likely related to a previous inscription.

EDCS-05000434.summary: The names of Appius Iunius Zethus, possibly as a dedication or owner.

EDCS-05000435.translation: Quintus Caius Antonius, the Antonian association of Corduba.
EDCS-05000435.summary: Records the name of a commercial or social association from Corduba, the Antonian association.

EDCS-05000441.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aebura, daughter of Reburrinus, aged 30.

EDCS-05000448.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Servenius Racilianus, who died at the age of two.

EDCS-05000459.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sarapiacus lived for 20 years.
EDCS-05000459.summary: This is a tombstone for Sarapiacus, who died at the age of twenty.

EDCS-05200314.summary: This is a tombstone for Caeno Aleonicus.

EDCS-05200315.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Her husband places her here, who lived for 40 years; may the earth rest lightly upon you.
