EDCS-24600709.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Aurelius Zenonis Ianuarius, likely a member of the college of the Six, around the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28300365.translation: Sisarius died on the 5th day before the Nones of October in the year 676 (AD 624), being about 26 years of age, and rests buried in this place, hoping for future resurrection in Christ. Amen.
EDCS-28300365.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sisarius, dated to AD 624, expressing Christian hope for resurrection.

EDCS-28400022.translation: Lucius Coilius Fuscus lived 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-28400022.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the name, lifespan, and burial of Lucius Coilius Fuscus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400024.translation: [...] may the earth lie lightly upon you. [...] caused this to be made.
EDCS-28400024.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription requesting a blessing for the deceased and indicating someone arranged for the burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400027.translation: Of 5,019 brave soldiers of the legion, whose labor bravely defended the old camp, this burial place for them, who have returned here, once, after long times, sensing that the eagles would return, the twentieth legion, whose former camp winter quarters these spirits guard.
EDCS-28400027.summary: This is a military epitaph honoring 5,019 soldiers of the Legio XX, commemorating their defense of a camp and the return of the legion's standards, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400028.translation: To the departed spirits, the bones of 5,019 soldiers of the legion, killed with Quintilius V, are collected here and rest; if the departed spirits have any further buried ashes and bones, and command their voices, they pray for the eagles returning happy and fortunate to their camp.
EDCS-28400028.summary: This military epitaph, likely associated with the Legio XX and the events involving Germanicus and Quintilius V, concerns the collection and resting place of soldiers' remains and expresses hopes for the legion's return, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400405.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Lucius Quintius Rufus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, gave this gift from his own money.
EDCS-28400405.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Lucius Quintius Rufus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28700484.summary: This is likely a tombstone or a dedication inscription mentioning the name Lucius Servilius Pollio Rufinus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28700495.translation: and of Marcus Valerius Cassianus, son of Marcus, of the Annian tribe, and of the Flavii Iulii Fabii Seneciani
EDCS-28700495.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to be part of a list of names, possibly related to family lineage or funerary honors, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701488.summary: This is a funerary inscription or dedication identifying Aulus Sabinius Aurelianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701495.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Regillus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701503.summary: This inscription, likely from the Visigothic period, is a fragmentary ownership or funerary marker with a repeated name.

EDCS-28701505.translation: From a pious Icone, in her own [space], 6 years [old].
EDCS-28701505.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records the short life of a person named Icone, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701506.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Maura, to her most loving companion, 30 years old, pious in her own [space], is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701506.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Maura at age 30, dedicated to her companion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701507.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Didyma, 18 years old, is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701507.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Didyma at age 18, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701508.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sextius, 22 years old, pious in his own [space], is laid here. May the earth be light for you. Sacred to the Divine Shades. December, 22 years old, pious in his own [space], is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701508.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of two individuals, Sextius and December, both aged 22, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701509.translation: Daphnus, 22 years old, is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701509.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Daphnus at age 22, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701575.translation: To Baebia Rustica, dear to her own people, 35 years old, is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701575.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Baebia Rustica at age 35, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701576.translation: Marcia, daughter of Marcius, Sextilla, is laid here.
EDCS-28701576.summary: This funerary epitaph identifies Marcia, daughter of Marcius, also known as Sextilla, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701577.translation: Quieta, 70 years old, dear to her own people, is laid here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-28701577.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Quieta at age 70, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701578.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lauxenusa, aged 75, dear to her own people; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-28701578.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lauxenusa, dating to the Roman Imperial period, stating her age and a common wish for the deceased.

EDCS-28701579.summary: A funerary epitaph for Teru, stating his age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701581.summary: A simple funerary epitaph naming Marcia Eutethyce, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701601.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Annia Prisca lived 24 years, dear to her own people.
EDCS-28701601.summary: A funerary epitaph for Annia Prisca, dating to the Roman Imperial period, indicating her age and beloved status.

EDCS-28701603.translation: Fabulla, daughter of Marcus, is placed here, dear to her own people.
EDCS-28701603.summary: A funerary epitaph for Fabulla, daughter of Marcus, stating she is buried here and loved by her family.

EDCS-28701604.translation: Iunia, daughter of Publius Manilius, aged 50, is placed here, may the earth lie lightly upon you, dear to her own people.
EDCS-28701604.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iunia, daughter of Publius Manilius, aged 50, indicating her burial and expressing wishes for her afterlife.

EDCS-28701605.translation: Saturnina, dear to her own people, aged 19, is placed here.
EDCS-28701605.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturnina, aged 19, stating she is buried here and was beloved by her family.

EDCS-28701606.translation: Zethus, dear to his own people, aged 14, is placed here, may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-28701606.summary: A funerary epitaph for Zethus, aged 14, stating his burial and expressing wishes for his afterlife.

EDCS-30000132.translation: Firmilia, slave of Marcus Granius, aged 50, is placed here, may the earth lie lightly upon you. Sura, her daughter, made this from her own means.
EDCS-30000132.summary: A funerary epitaph for Firmilia, a slave of Marcus Granius, erected by her daughter Sura, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000133.translation: To Mercury the Supreme God. [He/She] gladly paid a vow with a willing mind.
EDCS-30000133.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Mercury, recording the fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000135.summary: A dedication or notice mentioning Bishop Marcianus, likely from the 4th or 5th century CE.

EDCS-30000299.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the name and age of Gaius Cornelius, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-30000304.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the name and age of Gaius Traius, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-30000308.translation: Ianuaris, servant of God, lived about 41 years, passed away in peace on the 11th day before the Kalends of September, in the era 540.
EDCS-30000308.summary: A Christian tombstone recording the lifespan and death date of Ianuaris, a servant of God, dated to 540 CE (AD 482/483).

EDCS-30000309.translation: Quintus lived 19 years in this age... Altegia, a distinguished woman, lived about 40 years, passed away in peace on the 3rd day before the Nones of October, in the era 757.
EDCS-30000309.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespans of Quintus and Altegia, with Altegia noted as a distinguished woman, dated to 757 CE (AD 700/701).

EDCS-31600025.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Peregrinus, the actor, aged 60, pious in his family, lies here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-31600025.summary: A tombstone for Peregrinus, an actor who died at age 60, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31600065.translation: The municipium of Nundina... quinquennial official and priest of the province of Further Hispania... the most distinguished order...
EDCS-31600065.summary: A dedication by municipal officials and the local senate of Nundina, possibly related to a public building or monument in Further Hispania.

EDCS-31600066.translation: For Sergia Castellana, 4 pounds 7 ounces of scruples.
EDCS-31600066.summary: An inscription recording a weight of 4 pounds and 7 ounces for Sergia Castellana, possibly related to funerary offerings or a purchase.

EDCS-32601913.translation: Aelia Elina rejoices with her children in their offspring, greetings.
EDCS-32601913.summary: A dedication or greeting by Aelia Elina to her children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32601925.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the ownership or dedication by Gaius Iunius Draco, likely a tombstone or ownership marker.

EDCS-32601971.translation: Of Publius Mussidius Sempronianus, son of Publius Mussidius Sempronianus.
EDCS-32601971.summary: This is an ownership inscription for a funerary monument, likely a tomb or sarcophagus, belonging to Publius Mussidius Sempronianus, with a repeated name for emphasis or clarity.

EDCS-32602083.summary: This inscription records the workshop of a craftsman named Micio, possibly indicating ownership or origin of goods.

EDCS-32602084.summary: This is an ownership or dedication inscription, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Gaius Valerius Maximus.

EDCS-32602194.translation: Of Fabia, daughter of Marcus, Fabula.
EDCS-32602194.summary: This inscription records a woman named Fabula, daughter of Marcus, likely for a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-32602210.summary: This inscription records Lucius Munatius Philus, possibly as an owner of a monument or as a dedicant.

EDCS-32602212.summary: This inscription identifies Anteros, a freedman of Faustullus, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-32602213.summary: This inscription simply states the name Publius Conanus, most likely for a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-32602214.summary: This inscription records the workshop of Matthias and Blancus, possibly indicating ownership or origin of goods.

EDCS-32602251.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Fabricius Masclus, likely for a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-32602260.summary: This inscription records Gaius Vibius Claranus, likely as the owner of a monument or as a dedicant.

EDCS-32602267.summary: This is a simple ownership inscription, likely on a tombstone or a piece of property, identifying Cornelius Alexander as the owner.

EDCS-32602268.summary: This is a maker's inscription, stating that Cinnamus was the craftsman who produced the object.

EDCS-32602269.translation: Of Marcus Cornelius and Lucius Cornelius.
EDCS-32602269.summary: This is an ownership inscription, likely on a tombstone or property, indicating joint ownership by Marcus Cornelius and Lucius Cornelius.

EDCS-32602280.summary: This is a genitive inscription, possibly on a tombstone or property, identifying Potius Caius Valerius.

EDCS-32602462.summary: This inscription indicates the name and ownership of a workshop, belonging to Attus Paternus.

EDCS-32602475.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop belonging to Paternus.

EDCS-32602476.translation: The pottery works of Caius Sellesius.
EDCS-32602476.summary: This inscription identifies the pottery production facility owned by Caius Sellesius.

EDCS-32602477.summary: This inscription identifies the pottery production facility belonging to Sellesius.

EDCS-32803834.translation: To Rufius Sextus, by estimate, one pound and a half pound and a half pound.
EDCS-32803834.summary: This inscription records a dedication or a valuation concerning Rufius Sextus, specifying a weight of goods valued at one and a half pounds, likely related to trade or a funerary offering.

EDCS-33000041.translation: Here I remember having once had intercourse with a girl, in whose vagina I almost perished from the cold, and will be... whoever has a fundament possesses both...
EDCS-33000041.summary: This is a humorous and obscene inscription found on a tomb, written in a crude epigrammatic style, likely from the later Roman Empire, describing a sexual encounter.

EDCS-33000042.translation: The body of Servanda, after her funeral, also lies in this tomb; dedicated to the god, the virgin remained, standing with the sacred virgins in the convent in a noble assembly; for thirty years she flourished in body and soul; rapt above, she reigns in the heavenly court. She died on the twelfth day before the Kalends of June. From the time of death, 687 years.
EDCS-33000042.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a virgin named Servanda, who died in 687 AD and is buried in this tomb.

EDCS-33200024.translation: Basilia, servant of Christ, lived more or less thirty-five years and departed in peace on the day before the Nones of October in the year 568.
EDCS-33200024.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Basilia, a servant of Christ, who died in 568 AD.

EDCS-33200025.translation: Fuscus, son of Fucinus, from Talabriga, aged 22, is buried here.
EDCS-33200025.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fuscus, a 22-year-old man from Talabriga.

EDCS-33200026.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Iulius Reburrinus from Olisipo, aged 41, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly upon you. Fortunata, his companion, took care to have this made.
EDCS-33200026.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Iulius Reburrinus, a 41-year-old man from Olisipo, commissioned by his companion Fortunata.

EDCS-33200027.translation: Sutrio, son of Caleius, from Talabriga, aged 30.
EDCS-33200027.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sutrio, a 30-year-old man from Talabriga.

EDCS-33200031.translation: Lucius Iulius Campanus, son of Lucius, from Tarmesis, is buried here.
EDCS-33200031.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Iulius Campanus from Tarmesis.

EDCS-33200032.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Cornelius.

EDCS-33200033.translation: Boutia, daughter of Mandus, is buried here.
EDCS-33200033.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Boutia, daughter of Mandus.

EDCS-33200035.translation: Of Lucius Vibius Amarantus, 21 and a half feet.
EDCS-33200035.summary: This inscription likely records the burial length or the dimensions of a plot belonging to Lucius Vibius Amarantus.

EDCS-33200036.summary: This is likely a funerary inscription or a simple dedication for Avellius, son of Acallus.

EDCS-33400734.translation: Cattosa, a servant of God, lived more or less 70 years, departed in peace on the third day before the Ides of October in the year 552 (of the local era).
EDCS-33400734.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and death of a servant of God, Cattosa, dated to 552 (AD).

EDCS-33700002.translation: Lucius Flavius Faustus is buried here.
EDCS-33700002.summary: A simple funerary inscription identifying the deceased as Lucius Flavius Faustus.

EDCS-33700021.summary: This inscription consists of a triple repetition of the name Sextus, possibly a funerary marker or a playful inscription.

EDCS-33700037.summary: This inscription names Aulus Silus Acceptus, likely indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-33700039.summary: This inscription names Caius Iunius Dracontus, likely indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-33700048.translation: to Furiana, daughter of Numerius, wife of Optatus.
EDCS-33700048.summary: This inscription identifies Furiana, daughter of Numerius and wife of Optatus, likely a funerary or dedicatory text.

EDCS-33700055.translation: Sacred to Fontanus. Flavia Sedata, having paid her vow willingly and with a good heart, for the health of Caius Aufustius Vegetus.
EDCS-33700055.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the deity Fontanus by Flavia Sedata for the health of Caius Aufustius Vegetus.

EDCS-33700060.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Anneia Aciliana Arvensis, daughter of Marcus, aged 28, pious towards her own people, is buried here; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-33700060.summary: A funerary inscription for Anneia Aciliana Arvensis, detailing her parentage, age, piety, and including a wish for a light burial.

EDCS-33700062.translation: After death, Lucretia Lucilla, his wife, erected this.
EDCS-33700062.summary: A funerary inscription stating that Lucretia Lucilla, wife of the deceased, erected the monument after his death.

EDCS-33800131.translation: Sacred to Diana Augusta. Marcus Valerius Dexter, son of Lucius, a quinquennial duovir of the municipality, dedicated this sacred statue.
EDCS-33800131.summary: A dedication of a sacred statue to Diana Augusta by Marcus Valerius Dexter, who held a municipal office.

EDCS-33800133.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Acilius Sino, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-33800134.translation: Sacred to the Anataidae. The council of the municipality of Florentia (Iliberris) gave and dedicated this gift because of the benefits conferred upon the people, through Valeria Florentina, priestess.
EDCS-33800134.summary: This is a dedication by the municipal council of Florentia (Iliberris) to the Anataidae, honoring Valeria Florentina, the priestess who made the dedication, for her benefits to the people.

EDCS-33800139.translation: [For the] Emperor Antoninus Caesar Augustus, son of [---], son of the Divine ---, father of the fatherland, [dedicated by] ---
EDCS-33800139.summary: This is a fragmented inscription honoring Emperor Antoninus Caesar Augustus, likely part of a public dedication.

EDCS-33800140.translation: To the divine spirits. His body [lies here]. His mother [dedicated this]. The people and council of Florentia (Iliberris).
EDCS-33800140.summary: This is a dedication to the divine spirits for an individual, with the mother and the people and council of Florentia (Iliberris) mentioned.

EDCS-33800141.translation: Sacred to the greatest Hercules Victor, to the most holy Silvanus the Pacifier, the Unconquered, the Olympian, the Cubant, the Great Triumphator. The senate and people of Iliberris dedicate this on the Kalends of July, with Lucius Domitius and Appius Claudius as consuls.
EDCS-33800141.summary: This is a dedication by the senate and people of Iliberris to Hercules Victor and Silvanus the Pacifier, dated to the consulship of Lucius Domitius and Appius Claudius.

EDCS-33800143.translation: To Gaius Antistius, son of Gaius, Antistius Vetus, patriarch, for his kinsman Turpio of Illipula, who, having been raised to the highest rank, loyally and steadfastly defended the fatherland, having defeated the enemy in battle in the territory of Ilurconensis and having routed and put to flight [the enemy] near our city, [he restored] our city, which had collapsed with its walls and ancient buildings, by diverting water and conducting it through bridges built by collective effort, and by providing other necessary supplies for the city and province, for all the honors bestowed upon him by the republic and for his fatherly care for the citizens of Illipula, who once rivaled Rome in glory and Carthage, [he raised] a gilded statue with its base in the forum of Archimila, opposite the temple of Minerva, on the solemn day of the Quinquatria, the second day before the Ides of March, with Aimillius and Sextus Servilius as curators and consuls.
EDCS-33800143.summary: This extensive inscription honors Turpio of Illipula for his military achievements and civic contributions, detailing the construction of aqueducts and bridges and the erection of a gilded statue in his honor during the consulship of Aimillius and Sextus Servilius.

EDCS-33800144.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Hercules Augustus. The people and council of Iliberris dedicate and give this. Because of the amplification and propagation of the worship of the god or immortal gods. Love, desire, and Priapus, powerful propagator of the race, foster me. Sacred to you who make all living things. The people and council of Rome, with Marcus F.-- Cs. of Ilipula, in Rome.
EDCS-33800144.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter, Hercules, and Priapus by the people and council of Iliberris, celebrating the expansion of religious worship.

EDCS-33800145.translation: In the second year of Emperor Nero, on the Kalends of April, he suffered martyrdom in this place in Illipula. Chosen for this purpose, Saint Hiscius, disciple of the Apostle James, with his disciples Turilo, Panuncio, Maronio, Centulio, passed into eternal life seeking [martyrdom] through the fire, in which they were burned alive, so that their ashes, converted into stones, lie in the caverns of this sacred mountain, where, as reason demands, they should be venerated in their memory. In the second year of Emperor Nero's reign, on the Kalends of April, he suffered martyrdom in this place in Illipula. Divine Thesiphon, formerly called Abenathard, disciple of the divine James the Apostle, a man endowed with learning and holiness, wrote a book called 'The Foundation of the Church' on lead tablets, and his disciples Divine Maximinus and Luparius also suffered martyrdom, whose dust is with the dust of the divine martyrs in the caverns of this sacred mountain, that they may be venerated in their memory. G.C.P.C. of Florentini (Iliberitani). In the second year of Emperor Nero's reign, on the Kalends of February, he suffered martyrdom in this place in Illipula. Divine Caecilius, disciple of Saint James, a man endowed with learning in languages and holiness, commented on the prophecies of the Apostle John, which are placed with other relics in the upper part of the uninhabitable tower of Turpiana, as the disciples Divine Setentrius and Patritius, who suffered with him, told me, whose dust lies in the caverns of this sacred mountain, that they may be venerated in their memory. The burnt body of Divine Mesiton, martyr, suffered under Emperor Nero. Liber de Sensia Dei, which the disciple of the divine Apostle James, Thesiphon, wrote in his natural Arabic language in Solomonic characters. The book 'The Foundation of the Church' written in Solomonic characters.
EDCS-33800145.summary: This lengthy inscription details the martyrdom of several early Christians, including Saint Hiscius, Thesiphon, Caecilius, Mesiton, and their disciples, in Illipula during the reign of Emperor Nero, and mentions their relics and writings.

EDCS-33800147.translation: Iliberris. Vespasian. In honor of the Hierosolymitan war, delivered the human race.
EDCS-33800147.summary: This inscription from Iliberris honors Vespasian and references the war in Jerusalem, suggesting a connection to the Jewish War.

EDCS-34300001.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sotis lived 65 years. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34300001.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits for Sotis, who lived 65 years.

EDCS-34300002.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mars by an individual named Sequens.

EDCS-34300583.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a personal name or title.

EDCS-34300595.summary: This inscription seems to record the dedication of a seat, possibly by or for sons.

EDCS-34300596.summary: This is a name, Lucius Aemilius Senalus, possibly from a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-34300609.translation: Gaius Publilius Atticus and Gaius Publilius Herculanus, son of Gaius Publilius, of the Galeria tribe, gave the pavement as a gift.
EDCS-34300609.summary: This inscription records a gift of pavement to a public space by two individuals, Gaius Publilius Atticus and his son Gaius Publilius Herculanus.

EDCS-34300611.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Calpurnius Protus lived more or less 55 years and 3 months. He lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-34300611.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Calpurnius Protus, recording his age and a standard epitaph wishing him peace in the afterlife.

EDCS-34300885.translation: From the pottery works of Caesar.
EDCS-34300885.summary: This inscription indicates that the object it is on comes from an imperial pottery workshop.

EDCS-34300888.translation: His father accepted the honor and remitted the expense for Lucius Numisius Sollertes, son of Lucius Numisius, of the Sergia tribe, son of the decurions of the colony of Patricia.
EDCS-34300888.summary: This inscription records that the father of Lucius Numisius Sollertes accepted a public honor on his son's behalf and paid the associated costs, likely related to a magistracy in the colony of Patricia.

EDCS-34300892.translation: Of the colonists, from the calendar of Vegetianus.
EDCS-34300892.summary: This inscription seems to indicate a group affiliation or a date related to Vegetianus, possibly referring to colonists.

EDCS-34300893.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, mentioning imperial titles and a personal name, Sentius.

EDCS-34300894.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly honoring emperors, with an uncertain reading.

EDCS-34300947.translation: To Florentinus of Iliberri, patron, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-34300947.summary: This is a dedication to Florentinus of Iliberri, honoring him as patron by the town council.

EDCS-34301068.translation: Maurus, aged 30 years, lies here; may the earth be light for you. Maurus had it made at his own expense for his brother.
EDCS-34301068.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for an individual named Maurus, paid for by the deceased's brother.

EDCS-34301069.translation: Flavius Capito, son [of...], aged 28 years, lies here; may the earth be light for you.
EDCS-34301069.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Flavius Capito, recording his age and a wish for light earth.

EDCS-34301070.translation: Marcia Ephesia had it made at her own expense for her sister.
EDCS-34301070.summary: This is an epitaph indicating Marcia Ephesia commissioned a monument for her sister at her own expense.

EDCS-34400138.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Mula lived. Ulpia Acaicina made [this] for the well-deserving [mule]; may the earth be light for you.
EDCS-34400138.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a deceased mule named Mula, set up by Ulpia Acaicina.

EDCS-35200284.translation: I am Maria. My limbs shine; here is the urn. Having conquered the flesh with religious custom in sober fame, I am covered by a chaste celestial court and hall; here are hollow rocks.
EDCS-35200284.summary: This is a poetic epitaph for Maria, reflecting on her life, virtue, and presumed afterlife.

EDCS-35200425.translation: Highest honor to the holy Modefredus; may his memory flourish always in sports, with Cara and [her] sacred husband.
EDCS-35200425.summary: This inscription honors Modefredus and his wife Cara, possibly related to athletic or festive activities.

EDCS-37600957.translation: Cyprianus, lector of the Church of Hispalis, servant of Christ, lived about 40 years, departed in peace on the 12th day before the Kalends of February in the year 582 of the era.
EDCS-37600957.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cyprianus, a church lector in Hispalis, recording his death date in the Spanish era.

EDCS-38000019.translation: The Spirit here completes men with heavenly doctrine, and the Spirit himself teaches through the mouths of men. Go, ministers, fearing God, who carry sacred gifts in vessels for the rites. This path alone is suitable for heavenly ministries, as it separates the peoples by choruses in sacred rites. Whoever wishes to meditate on the praises and songs of Christ, submit prompt minds here to good studies. Here fear is given, here doctrine is given, here hearts are taught, here good thrives, from here evil falls. Here God is able to vivify men with pious flesh, blood, water, and sacred chrism. Peace glorifies those whom enemies had despised, and honor is more preferable to them than rejoicing in gladness. We have given these seats with perpetual praise. You [plural] bring servants into the future lot of the kingdom. This John, transgressing the heavens, found and reveals the word of the Father which he received from Christ's breast, worthy of the office of a virgin, who [himself] perennially received [the task] of keeping the mother of God in time.
EDCS-38000019.summary: This is a lengthy funerary inscription, likely poetic, commemorating John, who is praised for his spiritual understanding, teachings, and devotion, possibly as a minister or theologian.

EDCS-38000020.translation: This sacred cross bears the bodies of the blessed brothers Leander and Isidore, the first of the prophets. Florentina, their third sister, vowed to God forever, rests here, a worthy companion. Isidore separates the bodies of the two. Reader, inquire in books what kind of men they were and you will know they spoke all things well, certain in hope, full of faith, and above all chaste. See how the faithful grew through the teachings of the saints, and those whom impious laws held were returned to the Lord. As you look upon the painted images above, strive to see them living always. Leander, bishop of blessed memory, died on the 14th day before the Ides of March in the year 640. Isidore, bishop of holy memory, died on the 7th day before the Nones of April in the year 674. Florentina, vowed to God, died on the 5th day before the Kalends of September in the year 671.
EDCS-38000020.summary: This epitaph commemorates the burial of Bishops Leander and Isidore, and their sister Florentina, in 640, 674, and 671 respectively, on a cross-shaped monument.

EDCS-38000021.translation: Abundantia, servant of God, placed here by custom and venerated by name, look upon this high place, for it is ash. Behold, in the reign of the great Lord, she lived more or less [number] years.
EDCS-38000021.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Abundantia, a servant of God, with an incomplete lifespan.

EDCS-38000022.translation: Vigilia, of blessed memory, servant of God, lived more or less 21 years. She departed in peace on the 3rd day before the Nones of June in the year 565.
EDCS-38000022.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Vigilia, a servant of God, who died in 565.

EDCS-38000024.translation: Bulgaricus, servant of God, lived more or less 60 years. He departed in peace on the 8th day before the Kalends of June in the year 600.
EDCS-38000024.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Bulgaricus, a servant of God, who died in 600.

EDCS-38000026.translation: Aurelius Iulianus, of African origin, who lived 6 years, 10 months, and 11 days, remains in God's glory.
EDCS-38000026.summary: This epitaph records the death of Aurelius Iulianus, a child aged 6 years, 10 months, and 11 days.

EDCS-38000027.translation: In the name of the Lord Jesus Christ, consecration of the Lords Peter and Paul on the 12th day before the Kalends of June, in whose basilica rest the relics of the saints, namely Lady Maria, Lord Iulianus, Lord Stefanos, Lord Acisclus, Lord Laurentius, Lord Martinus, Lady Eulalia, Lord Vincentius, and three lords.
EDCS-38000027.summary: This inscription records the consecration of the basilica of Saints Peter and Paul on May 31st, listing the relics of various saints interred within.

EDCS-38000064.summary: This is a repetitive phrase, possibly a memorial or a chant, with no clear context.

EDCS-38000069.translation: Anni, who lived six years and five months, rests here in peace.
EDCS-38000069.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Anni, who died at the age of six years and five months.

EDCS-38000070.translation: While Mammet, king of the Saracens, reigned, Floresindus the deacon made this inscription.
EDCS-38000070.summary: This inscription records that Floresindus, a deacon, made a dedication during the reign of King Mammet of the Saracens.

EDCS-38000071.translation: In this place is held the tomb of Atana, a most distinguished man, whom ancient times called Gilda, born of the father Sinde, in the wondrous land of Beatia, John, uniquely called from the fount, wise, kind, moreover modest in speech, flourishing in the church with a quiet mind, orthodox, legitimately educated, may he be joined with blessed fate to the heavenly realms, having been placed with Christ, whom he devoutly worshipped, he will complete his course on the eighth day before the Ides of August, in the year 1673.
EDCS-38000071.summary: This epitaph commemorates Atana, also known as John Gilda, a wise and devout man, buried in 1673.

EDCS-38000075.translation: In this tomb rests Eresvide, an unworthy priest whose life was most honorable; he departed daily and rests on the 18th day of July, in the year 4019 (1019 AD).
EDCS-38000075.summary: This inscription records the burial of a priest named Eresvide in 1019 AD.

EDCS-38000077.translation: Martinus, a Christian and anointed with chrism, lies here covered by this stone, being upright.
EDCS-38000077.summary: This inscription commemorates Martinus, an anointed Christian.

EDCS-38000078.translation: The anointed limbs of Salvatus, properly buried, rest here, in the year 1075 (1075 AD) of his age.
EDCS-38000078.summary: This inscription records the burial of Salvatus in 1075 AD.

EDCS-38100228.translation: Marcus Petrucidius, son of Marcus, governor under Marcus Licinius.
EDCS-38100228.summary: This inscription records Marcus Petrucidius, a governor under Marcus Licinius.

EDCS-38100280.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Titus, son of Antonius, aged Gaius Lus, from Avona.
EDCS-38100280.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus, son of Antonius, who was from Avona.

EDCS-38100282.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulia Optata, daughter of Gaius, aged 70 years, is placed here; may the earth lie lightly on you. Her most dutiful [father/husband?] had it made.
EDCS-38100282.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Optata, aged 70, likely set up by a relative.

EDCS-38100289.summary: This inscription records Quintus Licinius, who held the office of duovir.

EDCS-38100294.translation: I.M.R. Cornulus, aged 19, LEF, is placed here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-38100294.summary: This funerary inscription records Cornulus, aged 19.

EDCS-38100295.translation: Marcus Cornelius Tam [e]ban to his brother from Faventina.
EDCS-38100295.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Marcus Cornelius Tam[e]ban to his brother.

EDCS-38100296.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Lucius Iulius Iulianus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-38100296.summary: This inscription records Lucius Iulius Iulianus fulfilling a vow to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-38100297.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely a tombstone, identifying Norbana as a potter and crediting Tolina with its creation.

EDCS-38100298.translation: Eratusa, 3 years and 5 months old. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100298.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young child named Eratusa, wishing them a light burial.

EDCS-38100299.translation: Primus Verna, 35 years old, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. Hermes had this made for his brother.
EDCS-38100299.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Primus Verna at age 35, with his brother Hermes commissioning the monument.

EDCS-38100303.translation: Alfidia Capra, 35 years old, and Alfidia Helpi, 21 years old, lie here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100303.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of two individuals, Alfidia Capra and Alfidia Helpi, with a wish for a light burial for both.

EDCS-38100305.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Gods and the Manes. Successianus, 9 years, 9 months, and 5 days old. His mother had this made. May he lie here with light earth upon him.
EDCS-38100305.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the gods of the underworld and the Manes, recording the death of Successianus at a young age and indicating his mother commissioned the monument.

EDCS-38100306.translation: To Lucius Arruntius Trophimus, a worthy man. M. Pacensi had this made. She lies here.
EDCS-38100306.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Lucius Arruntius Trophimus by M. Pacensi, who had the monument made for him.

EDCS-38100310.translation: Sacred to the Gods and Manes. Gratus, son of Vivi Secundus Nummus Erastus, a freedman, 25 years old, lies here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-38100310.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the gods and Manes, recording the death of Gratus, a 25-year-old freedman, with a wish for a light burial.

EDCS-38100311.translation: Lupicianus to himself and to Lucius Sulpicius and to Marcus Rubius Lucius Rubio [---].
EDCS-38100311.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording dedications made by Lupicianus to himself and at least two other individuals, with a partial lacuna.

EDCS-38700019.translation: In this altar are relics of Saint Stephen, fifteen in number: Stephen, Lucretia, Saturninus, Sebastian, Fructuosus, Augurius, Eulalia, Baudilus, Paulus the Confessor, Nazarius, Eulogius, Tirsus, Verissimus, Maxima, and Julia.
EDCS-38700019.summary: This inscription, likely from a reliquary or altar, lists fifteen relics of saints, including Saint Stephen, and other named individuals.

EDCS-38700021.translation: Here begins the martyrology of our lord Saint Stephen, of Saint John the Apostle.
EDCS-38700021.summary: This inscription marks the beginning of a martyrology, likely a text or document, dedicated to Saint Stephen and Saint John the Apostle.

EDCS-38700022.translation: Gunde Bebius, servant of God, lived for [unknown number of] years.
EDCS-38700022.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Gunde Bebius, a servant of God.

EDCS-38700023.translation: Martyria, untouched virgin, lived for 13 years and 5 months, received into peace on the day before the Kalends of January in the year [of the era].
EDCS-38700023.summary: This epitaph records the death of Martyria, a virgin, and her reception into peace on a specific date within an era.

EDCS-38700028.translation: [she/he] departed in the year 600 of the era.
EDCS-38700028.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a departure date within an era.

EDCS-38700029.translation: Here the body of the pious Optata is laid to rest. Optatus made this.
EDCS-38700029.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Optata, created by Optatus.

EDCS-38700030.translation: Ubitilde, servant of God, on the Ides of May in the year 500 of the era.
EDCS-38700030.summary: This inscription records the death of Ubitilde, a servant of God, on a specific date within an era.

EDCS-38700031.translation: Alexandria, most distinguished woman, lived for more or less 25 years, departed in peace on the tenth day before the Kalends of January in the year 583 of the era. Probus, her son, lived for two years and ten months, departed in peace.
EDCS-38700031.summary: This epitaph records the lives and peaceful deaths of Alexandria and her son Probus, with dates within an era.

EDCS-38700032.translation: Here are contained the relics of the saints, namely of Saint Stephen, Julian, Felix, Justus, Pastor, Fructuosus, Augurinus, Eulogius, Acisclus, Romanus, Martin, Quiricus, and Zoilus, martyrs. This basilica was dedicated on the seventeenth day before the Kalends of January in the second year of the pontificate of Pimen, in the year 668 of the era.
EDCS-38700032.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a basilica containing the relics of numerous martyrs during the pontificate of Pimen in an era.

EDCS-38700034.translation: In the name of the Lord, here are laid to rest the relics of the saints Servandus, Germanus, Saturninus, Justa, Rufina, martyrs, and John the Baptist, on the day of the Nones of June in the 33rd year of the reign of Pimen the pontiff, in the year 700 of the era.
EDCS-38700034.summary: This inscription records the burial of relics of martyrs and John the Baptist, during the pontificate of Pimen, in an era.

EDCS-38700036.translation: Here are the relics of the saints and martyrs, namely of Saint Thomas, Saint Dionysius, Saints Cosmas and Damian, Saint Sebastian, Saint Afra, and Saint Sabas.
EDCS-38700036.summary: This inscription lists the relics of several saints and martyrs housed in a location.

EDCS-38700037.translation: Zerezindo, duke, servant of God, lived for more or less 43 years, died on the third day before the Kalends of August in the year 616 of the era.
EDCS-38700037.summary: This epitaph records the life and death of Zerezindo, a duke and servant of God, with a date within an era.

EDCS-38700046.translation: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, here are the relics of the martyrs Vincent, Felix, and Julian. Dedication of this basilica on the Kalends of December, in the sixteenth year of the reign of Lord Pimen, bishop, in the era 682.
EDCS-38700046.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a basilica on December 1st, 682 (era), likely in the 16th year of Bishop Pimen's episcopate.

EDCS-38700047.translation: Urbana, a virgin, rests in the Lord.
EDCS-38700047.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Urbana, a virgin, who rests in the Lord.

EDCS-38700048.translation: A servant of God lived for half a year and departed in peace on the 15th day before the Kalends of March in the era 504.
EDCS-38700048.summary: This inscription records the death of a servant of God, who lived for six months and died on February 15th, 504 (era).

EDCS-38700049.translation: Rexina Cupari lived for 22 years and departed in peace.
EDCS-38700049.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rexina Cupari, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-38700091.summary: This is a short funerary inscription stating that Camilla died in God.

EDCS-38700103.translation: Here lies the distinguished Samuel, most illustrious, elegant in form, comely in stature, pleasant, who sang his office with the modulation of songs and charmed the hearts of all the common people listening, and lived in number six and ten years, namely eighteen, visited by the Lord, approved in this world, thus he departed from this world on the Sabbath day, and slept in the Lord, buried in this tomb at the third hour of the day, in the era nine hundred and sixty-nine, on the ninth day before the Kalends of December. Whoever reads this, look down upon the aforementioned great and pure presbyter and correct yourself.
EDCS-38700103.summary: This is a lengthy epitaph for Samuel, a priest, detailing his virtues, age (18), death date (November 23rd, 969 era), and a call for the reader to reflect and correct themselves.

EDCS-38700104.translation: In this place is laid to rest Amansvindus, a monk, honorable and magnificent, and fervent in charity, who was sober in mind, an excellent shepherd of Christ God and his sheep, like a brave warrior repelled the delights of the world, living four and ten years, namely forty-two, and having rested in the monastery, he departed to the world in this tomb, placed in the company of the assembly of confessors on the Kalends of January, the tenth day between the third hour and the crowing of the cocks, he slept on a Friday, in the era one hundred and ten times two and ten, while our Lord Jesus Christ reigns, Most High.
EDCS-38700104.summary: This inscription commemorates Amansvindus, a sober, charitable, and brave monk who died at age 42 and was buried in a tomb on January 1st, 220 (era), in the time of Christ's reign.

EDCS-38700111.translation: Over the tomb of Saint John the Confessor, John bore the prisons and dire iron chains for the love of Christ; here he rests, having fulfilled his duty in the court.
EDCS-38700111.summary: This inscription is placed over the tomb of Saint John the Confessor, who endured imprisonment and chains for Christ and is now at rest.

EDCS-38700112.translation: Epitaph of Saint Eulogius. Here lies Lepidus, martyr and shining doctor, Eulogius, a sweet light for the ages, a name who, blazing with zeal of faith, prior in virtue, ignited the spirits of great men, rigid in life and bright in his end, he did not perish in death but lives in an eternal dwelling. Believe me, I ask, he lives for the ages, he lives. Whoever rejoices in the heavenly glory of the kingdom, the prayer of Albari now I beg you, holy Recolas, that your name, fixed by the sweet friendship that held you here, may keep your friend Albari, the last, who, having passed through much calamity and not corrupted by vices, wanders through the devious paths of the world. May your prayer hold this fallen one to the pastures of life, that he may be joined in the love worthy of you, with whom he remained dear to you for ages. Grant it, God of gods, reigning through the ages. Amen.
EDCS-38700112.summary: This epitaph honors Saint Eulogius, a martyr and doctor, and also contains a prayer from Albari for another friend, also named Albari, asking for divine mercy and salvation, invoking Recolas and God.

EDCS-38700113.translation: Who, how great was Samson, the most famous abba, whose sacred remains rest in this urn. The land of Spain resounds, nourished by his words. Reader, now bend God with prayers, I beg you, bend the heavens, so that he may be able to ascend, cleansed of sins. He departed, far born, full of days. The month of August, indeed, on the twenty-first day. The month of August, indeed, on the twenty-first, in the era 928.
EDCS-38700113.summary: This inscription commemorates Samson, a highly regarded abba, whose relics are interred here, with prayers for his soul's ascent and a description of his death in August, on the 21st, in the year 928 of the era.

EDCS-38700114.translation: Victrix, our strength, after the crowds of the flesh are lulled to sleep, the fertile mother, overcoming the brutal, seeks to tear us away, living for ever through your merits in heaven, the martyr Eugenia, joining the senate of the saints, shines in the citadel, with Mercury struck down, circling the glory of the bloodied King, grants her a crown for future ages. You too, martyr, by divine command, send us forth. In this air, nine hundred and sixty-one are slain, seven days before the Kalends of April.
EDCS-38700114.summary: This is a hagiographical inscription commemorating the martyr Eugenia, detailing her death and heavenly reward, dated to 961 years after some era, seven days before the Kalends of April.

EDCS-38700115.translation: Samson the abbot offers this gift in the house of Saint Sebastian, martyr of Christ, in the year 963.
EDCS-38700115.summary: This is a dedication inscription by Samson the abbot, dated to the year 963, for the house of Saint Sebastian.

EDCS-38700116.translation: Cisclus, servant of God, died on the 3rd day before the Kalends of April, in the year 910.
EDCS-38700116.summary: This is an epitaph for Cisclus, a servant of God, dated to the year 910.

EDCS-38700117.translation: Vite didicus, servant of God, wife of Saracinus, died on the 20th day before the Kalends of August, in the year 900.
EDCS-38700117.summary: This is an epitaph for Vite didicus, servant of God and wife of Saracinus, dated to the year 900.

EDCS-38700118.translation: Here lies Milena, bishop of Biterrae, who served the fatherland in the year 1000.
EDCS-38700118.summary: This is an epitaph for Milena, bishop of Biterrae, who served the fatherland, dated to the year 1000.

EDCS-38700119.translation: Here lies Nomiemb, bishop of Biterrae, SEM LOIMNA FLA FE if he served the fatherland for the glory of his descendants, in the year 1000.
EDCS-38700119.summary: This is an epitaph for Nomiemb, bishop of Biterrae, who served the fatherland, dated to the year 1000.

EDCS-38800059.translation: Antonia Vetia lived for 11 years and 8 months.
EDCS-38800059.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Antonia Vetia.

EDCS-38800060.translation: In the name of the Lord, Guisuifrondina lived six years, ten months, and departed in the year 722, four days before the Kalends of December. Emeritensis Frontinus, her husband, often, although ill, [rested] in peace.
EDCS-38800060.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Guisuifrondina, recording her death in the year 722 and mentioning her husband, Emeritensis Frontinus.

EDCS-38800063.translation: Aurelius Felix, a free-born Roman citizen of Carteia, lived for 31 years, 7 months, and 16 days; sweet of soul, accepted by all, [rests] in peace.
EDCS-38800063.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Felix, a citizen of Carteia, recording his lifespan and peaceful rest.

EDCS-38800064.translation: Firmana, an infant, sweet of soul, lived in good [health] for two years and eight months; she was gathered in peace on the 7th day before the Kalends of February, on a Saturday.
EDCS-38800064.summary: This is a funerary inscription for the infant Firmana, recording her brief life and peaceful repose.

EDCS-38800065.translation: Flavianus lived in Christ more or less 50 years, 50 years. He received the indulgence of the Lord's Supper through penitence, a servant of God. He died and rests in peace on the third day before the Kalends of April in the year 674.
EDCS-38800065.summary: This epitaph records the death and resting place of Flavianus, a servant of God, who died in the year 674.

EDCS-38800066.translation: Principius, a servant of God, the only son of the most distinguished man Emiliani and the illustrious woman Paulinae, lived seven years and ten months. He departed in peace on the fourth day before the Ides of May in the year 580. He was laid to rest on the eleventh day before the Kalends.
EDCS-38800066.summary: This epitaph records the death and burial of Principius, son of Emiliani and Paulinae, who died in the year 580.

EDCS-38800067.translation: Evasius, a servant of God, lived more or less 63 years. He departed in peace on the 12th day before the Kalends of June in the year 647.
EDCS-38800067.summary: This epitaph records the death of Evasius, a servant of God, who died in the year 647.

EDCS-38800085.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription likely naming a person and their connection to prefects.

EDCS-38800100.translation: Saxo Ferreus, son of Caelus Aurelius.
EDCS-38800100.summary: This is a funerary inscription naming Saxo Ferreus and identifying his father.

EDCS-38800102.translation: Son of Saxo Ferreus, son of I C F Pa.
EDCS-38800102.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription likely naming a person and their parentage.

EDCS-38800109.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Marcus Aemilius Rusticus.

EDCS-38800110.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Marcus Aemilius Rusticus.

EDCS-38800111.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Marcus Aemilius Rusticus.

EDCS-38800112.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Marcus Aemilius Rusticus.

EDCS-38800113.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800115.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800116.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800117.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800135.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800137.summary: This is a Roman name, likely appearing on a funerary monument or as an owner's name.

EDCS-38800157.translation: Of Lucius(?) Crescentis, from Cufens, M.
EDCS-38800157.summary: This inscription likely records the name of an individual, possibly a freedman or a soldier, associated with Cufens.

EDCS-38800158.translation: Of Lucius(?) Crescentis, from Cufens, S.
EDCS-38800158.summary: This inscription likely records the name of an individual, possibly a freedman or a soldier, associated with Cufens.

EDCS-38800160.translation: Of Gaius F. (son of?) from Cufens, M.
EDCS-38800160.summary: This inscription likely records the name of an individual, possibly a freedman or a soldier, associated with Cufens.

EDCS-38800174.translation: Of Quintus Fulvius Rusticus, the doctor.
EDCS-38800174.summary: This inscription records the name of Quintus Fulvius Rusticus, identified as a doctor, likely on a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-38800176.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Fulvius Rusticus, a freedman, likely on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-38800177.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Fulvius Rusticus, a freedman, likely on a tomb or monument, with slight variation from EDCS-38800176.

EDCS-38800179.summary: This inscription likely names Quintus Fulvius Nic..., possibly the beginning of a full name or title, and is incomplete.

EDCS-38800191.summary: This inscription begins with the name Gaius Iulius Albinus, and is incomplete.

EDCS-38800206.summary: This inscription refers to four individuals named Memmius from Rivense, potentially a family or group.

EDCS-38800264.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or ownership mark belonging to Lucius Valerius Trophimus.

EDCS-38900237.translation: Ianuarius, a servant of Christ, lived more or less for years and departed in peace on the Kalends of November in the era of 703; I commend my spirit into your hands, Lord.
EDCS-38900237.summary: This is a Christian epitaph dating to 703 AD (likely the Seleucid era), commemorating Ianuarius, a servant of Christ.

EDCS-39000003.summary: This inscription possibly describes a young person named Nico, distinguished by a ring, perhaps on a seal or as a marker.

EDCS-39200761.translation: Claudius Silvanus, patron and benefactor of the community of Syrians and [others] in Malaca, erected this [monument] for Claudius Niger, his patron and protector.
EDCS-39200761.summary: This bilingual inscription, partly in Greek, records the erection of a monument by Claudius Silvanus for his patron and benefactor, Claudius Niger, within the Syrian community of Malaca.

EDCS-39700083.translation: To Fabia Prisca, daughter of Gnaeus, from Asidonia; Fabius Seneca and Valeria Prisca, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-39700083.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Fabia Prisca, dedicated by Fabius Seneca and Valeria Prisca.

EDCS-39700084.translation: To the Infernal Deities. This monument accompanies the old bones of Decius Quirinus, who in his life saw 106 solar cycles. From the time of his son's marriage, 11; grandsons, 40; great-grandsons, 90. I ask that the earth be light to you. Funeral plot in front of the road, 14 feet; land, 16 feet.
EDCS-39700084.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Decius Quirinus, recording his lifespan, descendants, and the dimensions of his burial plot.

EDCS-39700090.translation: To Publius, son of [---], 15 years old, duovir, 50 years old.
EDCS-39700090.summary: This inscription likely records the age and possibly a magistracy of a young man named Publius.

EDCS-39700092.translation: Aius, 3 years old, is buried here. May the earth be light to you.
EDCS-39700092.summary: A simple epitaph for a child named Aius.

EDCS-39700097.translation: Annius Optatus, second of the allies, of the Juniores Avitores, from Iliturgi.
EDCS-39700097.summary: This inscription appears to record the affiliation of Annius Optatus with a military or civic group.

EDCS-39700127.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely referring to a workshop or a maker's mark.

EDCS-39700128.translation: Publius Grattius Aristocles of Malaga, with his wife Pompeia Philocyria, gave and dedicated four columns with their bases, at the request of the people.
EDCS-39700128.summary: This inscription records the dedication of four columns and their bases by Publius Grattius Aristocles and his wife.

EDCS-39700164.translation: lius Avitus, 50 years old, dutiful to his own, is buried here. May the earth be light to you. Fructuosa.
EDCS-39700164.summary: This epitaph records the burial oflius Avitus, noted for his piety, and includes a dedication by Fructuosa.

EDCS-40400135.translation: Florentius, a holy man, rested in peace on the seventh day before the Kalends of March, lived 53 years and was buried on the third day before the Ides of March, era 523.
EDCS-40400135.summary: This Christian epitaph records the death and burial dates and lifespan of Florentius.

EDCS-40400136.translation: Eulalius, servant of God, lived approximately 93 years, departed in peace in era 653.
EDCS-40400136.summary: This Christian epitaph records the lifespan and peaceful departure of Eulalius, a servant of God.

EDCS-40400138.translation: Here rests in the Lord Justus, a servant of Christ, the abbot, who lived 60 years, 5 months, and 3 days. He died on the 6th day before the Kalends of June in the year 581 [of the Spanish Era].
EDCS-40400138.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Justus, an abbot, who died in 581.

EDCS-41300125.translation: To the Libyan Hercules, unconquered god, the city of Martis [dedicates] a silver statue, with public funds.
EDCS-41300125.summary: This is a dedication of a silver statue to the god Hercules by the city of Martis.

EDCS-41300126.translation: You are called a column from the ancient, most famous cliff of Hercules, bearing a name from a noble lineage.
EDCS-41300126.summary: This is a poetic inscription on a column associated with Hercules.

EDCS-41300133.translation: To Hercules the Unconquered God and patron of the Republic of Aruccitanus, a bronze statue is dedicated by Secundus, treasurer of the temple and Aruccitanus, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-41300133.summary: This is a dedication of a bronze statue to Hercules by Secundus for the Republic of Aruccitanus.

EDCS-41300134.translation: To Marcus Atterius Paullinus, son of Marcus, who, when the tumult of the Baetica war arose, bravely performed many services for the Republic of Aruccitanus in maintaining the war, the old and young men of Aruccis [dedicate this] to their excellent citizen.
EDCS-41300134.summary: This is a dedication by the citizens of Aruccis to Marcus Atterius Paullinus for his military service in the Baetica war.

EDCS-41300145.translation: Because Favula gave it to her own household gods.
EDCS-41300145.summary: This is a dedication by Favula to her household gods.

EDCS-41300146.translation: The city of Ulia, by the tribunes and aediles, consuls, the leading men, [dedicates this] with great effort for the most strenuous support of the Jews and Iberians, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-41300146.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the city of Ulia to support the Jews and Iberians.

EDCS-41300147.translation: To C. Lavreceptus and his companions, and to Statius.
EDCS-41300147.summary: This is a dedication to C. Lavreceptus and his companions, and Statius.

EDCS-41300148.translation: To the departed spirits. Fabia L. n. and her parents dedicate this memorial stone. She lived 48 years. Here she rests.
EDCS-41300148.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Fabia, set up by her parents.

EDCS-41300149.translation: In the second year of Emperor Nero, on the Kalends of February, the martyr Cecilius, a disciple of the blessed James, suffered martyrdom in this place.
EDCS-41300149.summary: This inscription records the martyrdom of Saint Cecilius in the second year of Emperor Nero's reign.

EDCS-41300150.translation: In the second year of Emperor Nero, on the Kalends of March, the martyr Hiscius, a disciple of the Apostle James, elected for this purpose in this place of Ilipula, suffered martyrdom with his disciples Turilo, Panuncio, Maronio, and Centulio, passing through fire, by which they were burned alive, seeking eternal life, as if they had been turned into stones and then into lime, whose dust lies in the caverns of this sacred mountain, who, as reason dictates, should be venerated in their memory.
EDCS-41300150.summary: This inscription commemorates the martyrdom of Hiscius and his disciples in Ilipula during the second year of Nero's reign.

EDCS-41300151.translation: In the second year of Emperor Nero's reign, on the Kalends of April, the divine Thesiphon, formerly called Abenathar, a disciple of the divine Apostle James, a man endowed with learning and holiness, suffered martyrdom in this place of Ilipula, and with him his disciples the divine Maximinus and Luparius suffered martyrdom, whose dust is with the dust of the divine martyrs in the caverns of this sacred mountain, that they might be venerated in their memory, by G. C. P. C. Florens of Illiberis.
EDCS-41300151.summary: This inscription records the martyrdom of Thesiphon and his disciples in Ilipula during the second year of Nero's reign, with a dedication by Florens of Illiberis.

EDCS-41300152.translation: The body of the divine martyr Mesitonus was burned under Emperor Nero, the powerful.
EDCS-41300152.summary: This very brief inscription records the burning of the body of the martyr Mesitonus under Emperor Nero.

EDCS-41300153.translation: The book on the essence of God, which the divine Thesiphon, disciple of the Apostle James, wrote in one natural Arabic language in the characters of Solomon.
EDCS-41300153.summary: This inscription describes a book authored by Thesiphon, a disciple of James, written in Arabic using Solomon's characters.

EDCS-41300154.translation: The book of the foundation of the church, written in the characters of Solomon.
EDCS-41300154.summary: This inscription refers to a book detailing the foundation of a church, written in Solomon's characters.

EDCS-41300174.translation: Urban inscription of the Pantheon of Marcus Agrippa.
EDCS-41300174.summary: This is a short inscription likely indicating ownership or a dedication related to the Pantheon of Marcus Agrippa.

EDCS-41300175.translation: To the divine Titus Vespasianus Augustus, son of the divine Vespasian, Emperor, and to Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, victor of the human race, seven times consul, father of the country, and to the greatest Jupiter, and to Jerusalem conquered, Publius Verus Pollio, consul, dedicates this on behalf of himself and his gods.
EDCS-41300175.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Titus, Emperor Domitian, Jupiter, and commemorating the conquest of Jerusalem, made by Publius Verus Pollio during his consulship.

EDCS-41300176.translation: Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, consul for the third time, made [this]. Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune of the Potestas for the eleventh time, Consul for the third time, Father of the Country, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Tribune of the Potestas for the fifth time, Consul, Proconsul, restored the Pantheon from its old age with all its adornment.
EDCS-41300176.summary: This inscription details the original construction of the Pantheon by Marcus Agrippa and its restoration by Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-41300177.translation: To Lucius Annaeus Seneca of Corduba, for his services, the people of Nescanienses gratefully give.
EDCS-41300177.summary: This inscription is a grateful dedication by the people of Nescanienses to Lucius Annaeus Seneca of Corduba for his benefactions.

EDCS-41300179.summary: This is a very brief dedication to the goddess Diana at a location named Atalante.

EDCS-41300183.translation: Gaius Julius Caesar, son of the divine Julius, Augustus, great pontiff, grandson of Pompeius Magnus, Bautulus, Ignipedus with Bavlida, Cromarus, the great Celus, and Ptepales with Pimpinelida.
EDCS-41300183.summary: This inscription lists individuals, likely patrons and clients or members of a household, possibly related to public works or social organisation, with prominent figures like Caesar and Pompeius mentioned, suggesting a Republican or early Imperial date.

EDCS-41700176.translation: The Senate and People of Hispalis to the founder of the city of Hispalis.
EDCS-41700176.summary: This is a dedication by the Senate and People of Hispalis to the founder of their city, likely commemorating an individual or a benefaction.

EDCS-43100036.translation: Lucius, son of Augustas, to Nemesis. Zosimus.
EDCS-43100036.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Lucius, son of Augustas, with Zosimus acting as the dedicator or scribe, likely from Roman Egypt.

EDCS-43900006.translation: To the gods and shades. Valeria Pronima lived 46 years; she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-43900006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valeria Pronima, recording her age at death and expressing a common wish for the deceased.

EDCS-44100096.summary: This is a simple greeting or perhaps an epitaph for Aulus Licinius Canus.

EDCS-44100097.translation: Megale, his freedwoman, dedicated this to Quintus Avillius, son of Quintus, Papirius Valerianus.
EDCS-44100097.summary: This is a dedication by a freedwoman, Megale, to her former master, Quintus Avillius Papirius Valerianus, likely on a monument or tomb.

EDCS-44100098.translation: Megale, his freedwoman, dedicated this to Quintus Avillius, son of Quintus, Papirius Cornelianus.
EDCS-44100098.summary: This is a dedication by a freedwoman, Megale, to her former master, Quintus Avillius Papirius Cornelianus, likely on a monument or tomb.

EDCS-44100101.translation: Gaius Canuleius Faustinus, 58 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-44100101.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Canuleius Faustinus, recording his name and age at death.

EDCS-44100102.translation: Fabiana, the mother, placed this for her son Superstites, son of Rusticus, 17 years old. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-44100102.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother, Fabiana, for her deceased son, Superstites, noting his age and expressing a wish for his repose.

EDCS-44100103.translation: Paentus, son of Clodius, of the Clamena tribe, lived 33 years; she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-44100103.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Paentus, daughter of Clodius, recording her age at death, tribal affiliation, and a wish for her repose.

EDCS-44100104.translation: Cornelia Nymphe, freedwoman of Quintus, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. Gaius Pomponius Statius, a pleasant man, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you. And
EDCS-44100104.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelia Nymphe and Gaius Pomponius Statius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-44800419.translation: Pius, aged 51, may the earth lie lightly on him here within.
EDCS-44800419.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Pius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800420.translation: Traveller, all the supplies beyond the thresholds.
EDCS-44800420.summary: A brief, enigmatic inscription likely related to a tomb or a threshold, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800454.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caesia Tyche lived 12 years; she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-44800454.summary: A funerary inscription for Caesia Tyche, a young girl, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800455.translation: Mnester, son, aged 10, may the earth lie lightly on him here.
EDCS-44800455.summary: A funerary epitaph for a boy named Mnester, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800462.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning a freedman of Quintus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800485.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Petrucidius, son of Marcus, legate pro praetore of Marcus Licinius.
EDCS-44800485.summary: A dedication mentioning Marcus Petrucidius as legate pro praetore of Marcus Licinius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800493.translation: Marcus Petrucidius, son of Marcus, legate pro praetore of Marcus Licinius.
EDCS-44800493.summary: A dedication mentioning Marcus Petrucidius as legate pro praetore of Marcus Licinius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44800495.translation: aged 100, dutiful towards your family, she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-44800495.summary: A funerary epitaph for a woman who lived to 100, emphasizing her piety, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-45100128.translation: Lyde is buried here, 12 feet in front, 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-45100128.summary: A funerary inscription specifying the burial plot dimensions for Lyde, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-45100130.translation: Sapatio, servant of God, lived 31 years, departed in peace on the fifth day before the Ides of February, in the era 667.
EDCS-45100130.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sapatio, who died in the era 667.

EDCS-45100400.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Here lies Iunius, freedman of Marcius, aged [unknown], may the earth be light upon him.
EDCS-45100400.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Iunius, freedman of Marcius.

EDCS-45400001.translation: Of Pertinax, consul for the second time, from Siarensis.
EDCS-45400001.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Pertinax, who was consul for the second time.

EDCS-45400006.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. G. Apuleius and Nymphidia, unhappy parents, have made this for their son, Nymphidius Diocles, with his father G. Apuleius and grandfather G. Apuleius, the best of young men, who, while the people acclaimed him, was victorious in sacred contests, but was overcome by the same mournful fate; condemned to perpetual darkness and daily wailing. Farewell, soul, farewell, solace. He lived 19 years, 8 months, 50 days. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-45400006.summary: A funerary epitaph from unhappy parents for their son, Nymphidius Diocles, who died after achieving victory in sacred contests.

EDCS-45500013.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Primus lived 53 years, here he lies. I ask you, passerby, as you go, say: May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-45500013.summary: A funerary epitaph for Primus, who lived 53 years.

EDCS-45500035.translation: Isidore, may you live! Optata, may you live!
EDCS-45500035.summary: A brief inscription expressing wishes for life, possibly for Isidore and Optata.

EDCS-45900234.translation: Marcius, son of Ius, L. Manilius made this.
EDCS-45900234.summary: A funerary epitaph indicating that L. Manilius commissioned this for Marcius, son of Ius.

EDCS-45900238.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Apr, identified as a tent-mate, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-46400958.translation: Publius Caecilius Classicus, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-46400958.summary: An inscription identifying Publius Caecilius Classicus as the freedman of Publius.

EDCS-46600195.summary: This inscription likely records a quantity of 22 modii related to Pauli's journey or provisions.

EDCS-46600201.summary: Fragmentary inscription likely commemorating Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-46600202.summary: Fragmentary inscription likely commemorating Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-48700119.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Read if you wish. Heliodorus, the madman from Carthage, ordered me to be interred in this sarcophagus by his will, so that I might see if anyone madder than I would penetrate these places to see me.
EDCS-48700119.summary: A funerary epitaph from Carthage, likely from the Roman Imperial period, where the deceased expresses his madness and challenges visitors to find someone madder than himself.

EDCS-48800524.translation: Quintus Annius Quirinus Niger, through the favor of the Emperor Caesar Augustus, by virtue of his tenure as duovir, provided for the construction of this monument to Venus Victrix and gave it as a gift from his own funds.
EDCS-48800524.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of a monument to Venus Victrix, funded by Quintus Annius Quirinus Niger during his term as duovir, with the approval of the Emperor.

EDCS-48900613.translation: Cordyla Portensis, an old woman from Lixitas, excellent, aged 3 years.
EDCS-48900613.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl named Cordyla Portensis, noting her age and place of origin.

EDCS-49300082.translation: Witty Lixitana vetus excellens summarum, three years old.
EDCS-49300082.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl from Lixitas, described as witty and aged three.

EDCS-49300167.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Primitivus lived for 50 years, pious towards his own; Aurelius Piramus made this for you, may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-49300167.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Primitivus, erected by Aurelius Piramus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300168.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Polytimus.
EDCS-49300168.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the deceased named Polytimus.

EDCS-49300169.summary: A dedication or funerary marker for Ancharia Inventa, possibly indicating a second individual with the same name or a second inscription.

EDCS-49300476.translation: Lucius Valerius, son of Lucius, from Numeria.
EDCS-49300476.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius, son of Lucius, likely a Roman citizen identified by his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-49800409.summary: This inscription names Appeanus, son of Marcianus, and describes his profession as a healer.

EDCS-51400905.translation: Tuccudius, aged 18, is buried here; his mother had this made.
EDCS-51400905.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tuccudius, aged 18, with the monument commissioned by his mother.

EDCS-51400908.translation: To Gaius Servius Rufus Terentianus, proconsul, patron.
EDCS-51400908.summary: A dedication to Gaius Servius Rufus Terentianus, honoring him as proconsul and patron.

EDCS-51400909.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caia Aemilia lived 22 years and one day. Her sister Aelia, dutiful, placed this; she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-51400909.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caia Aemilia, who lived 22 years and one day, placed by her sister Aelia, with a standard wish for the deceased.

EDCS-51400910.translation: From Turannus the administrator, to our Flaccus.
EDCS-51400910.summary: A dedication from an administrator named Turannus to someone called Flaccus.

EDCS-51900001.summary: A dedication to the goddess Felicitas (Fortune) by Urtialia Aplidovi.

EDCS-52100108.translation: Of Diophantus, son of Bouchatius; of Diophantus, son of Bouchatius.
EDCS-52100108.summary: This inscription seems to be a repetition of names, possibly for identification or property marking, identifying Diophantus, son of Bouchatius.

EDCS-52100111.translation: Greater than gold and silver are immortal gifts to Artemis, and far superior to the hunt; to bring gifts to the head from the gods is not pious; Ariannus, the proconsul, [dedicates this] to his guests.
EDCS-52100111.summary: An inscription by the proconsul Ariannus, possibly on a public monument or religious offering, contrasting material gifts with divine ones and dedicating it to guests.

EDCS-53300311.translation: Valerius Viator is buried here; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-53300311.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Valerius Viator, with a common wish for the deceased.

EDCS-53300312.translation: Fabia Pusinna, freedwoman of Lucius, lies here; Fulvius Staturus Nuius Starus [is perhaps the dedicator or another buried person].
EDCS-53300312.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fabia Pusinna, a freedwoman, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-53300315.translation: Lucius Fabius Catulinus, son of Lucius, [erected this] for Aniketa, daughter of Lucius, his sister.
EDCS-53300315.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Lucius Fabius Catulinus to his sister Aniketa, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-53300321.translation: Mourn me, all you whom the earth contains, may your own waters wash [you as I have washed you], so may the merciful Christ forgive you your debts, whom I clothed, now the earth presses me down; neither silver nor shining gold helps me. He who is called [died]. He died aged more or less 3 months. He departed in peace on the III days before the Kalends of [a month] in the year 70 of the era.
EDCS-53300321.summary: This is a funerary inscription with Christian connotations, lamenting the deceased and emphasizing divine forgiveness, possibly from late antiquity or the early Byzantine period.

EDCS-53300322.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a maker's mark or ownership statement, potentially for a crafted object or building, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701685.translation: Sacred [dedication]. Publius Porcius Sisenninus, aedile, twice a duumvir, dedicated this gift from his own money.
EDCS-55701685.summary: This is a dedication of a gift funded by Publius Porcius Sisenninus, who held the offices of aedile and duumvir, likely from a Roman colony in the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701686.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, possibly related to Marcus Valerius Maila, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701688.translation: Publius Fabius Athenodorus, doctor, [belonging to the] Sergia [tribe].
EDCS-55701688.summary: This inscription records Publius Fabius Athenodorus, identified as a doctor and possibly belonging to the Sergia tribe, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701689.translation: The wise doctor Diopithes was worthy to live forever.
EDCS-55701689.summary: This is an epitaph for a doctor named Diopithes, expressing a wish for his eternal life, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701690.translation: Apollonius, aged 30, is buried here; may the earth always be light for you, well-deserving one.
EDCS-55701690.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Apollonius, who died at the age of 30, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701691.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name and filiation, possibly an ownership mark or part of a larger dedication, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-55701692.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Valerius Max.
EDCS-55701692.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Manes by Valerius Max.

EDCS-58900609.translation: To Marcus N. Calvisius, aged 70, and Lucius N. Aper, aged 41. Aemilia Anulla, daughter of Quintus, had this made for her husband and son. Marcus N. Maxumus requests that any passerby say: 'May the earth be light upon you.'
EDCS-58900609.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus N. Calvisius and Lucius N. Aper, commissioned by Aemilia Anulla, with a common 'sit tibi terra levis' formula.

EDCS-59500358.translation: To Quintus Vibius, son of Quintus, grandson of Marcus, great-grandson of Gnaeus.
